listings.name: LibreOffice
listings.realcost: Nothing. Open source, donation-funded.
free_includes: The full suite: Writer, Calc, Impress, Draw, Base
Opens and saves Word, Excel, and PowerPoint formats
PDF export built in
Unlimited use, offline, no account, forever
listings.short: Yes — completely. LibreOffice is a full office suite (documents, spreadsheets, presentations) that you download and own. No account, no card, no trial clock.
listings.catch: There is no catch. The interface feels a generation older than Microsoft 365, and heavy .docx files can shift formatting slightly — that's the whole trade.
listings.worth: ≈ $100+ a year — roughly the Microsoft 365 subscription you're not paying.
smart_moves: Download only from libreoffice.org — lookalike sites wrap the free installer in junk
Save as .docx or .xlsx when sharing with Office users to avoid formatting drift
If any store charges for it, that's a repackager — the real thing is always free
listings.get_summary: In practice: you can run a home office, write a book, and keep the family budget on this alone — no subscription, ever.
drawbacks: Interface looks a generation older than Microsoft 365
Complex .docx files can shift formatting
No built-in cloud sync — pair it with any drive you like
more_info: Why is it free? Who pays?

LibreOffice is maintained by The Document Foundation, a nonprofit funded by donations and corporate contributors. There's no data business and no premium version being dangled — the software is the whole product, given away on principle. That's why the free-ness is structural rather than a marketing phase.
Will my Word documents survive?

It opens and saves .docx and .xlsx well. Simple documents transfer cleanly; heavily formatted files — intricate text boxes, macros, exotic fonts — can shift a little. For anything layout-critical going to someone else, export a PDF and the question disappears.
Downloading safely

The one real hazard is fake download portals that wrap the free installer in adware. Type libreoffice.org yourself. Anything charging money for LibreOffice, including in app stores, is a repackager selling you free software.

listings.name: GIMP
listings.realcost: Nothing. Open source since 1996.
free_includes: Layers, masks, channels, and selection tools
Full filter library plus third-party plugins
Every export format, no watermarks
Scripting and batch processing
listings.short: Yes. GIMP is a professional-grade image editor — layers, masks, filters, plugins — with no account, no watermark, and no paid version hiding above it.
listings.catch: The catch is the learning curve, not the price. It's genuinely free forever, but Photoshop muscle memory won't transfer cleanly.
listings.worth: ≈ $270+ a year — Photoshop's single-app price, at zero.
smart_moves: Official site only: gimp.org — fake installers are the one real risk
Learn from GIMP-specific tutorials; Photoshop guides will fight you
Free Photoshop-style layout themes exist if the default interface hurts
listings.get_summary: In practice: real photo retouching and graphic design with the full professional toolset — once you climb its hill.
drawbacks: Steep learning curve; Photoshop habits don't transfer
No nondestructive editing workflow like Lightroom
Some professional print (CMYK) work needs plugins
more_info: Why is it free?

GIMP is a GNU project built by volunteers over nearly three decades. There is no company, no upsell, and no premium edition — which also explains the rough edges: the roadmap runs on contributor time, not revenue targets.
Can it actually replace Photoshop?

For photo retouching, compositing, and web graphics — largely yes, once relearned. Where it lags: a nondestructive editing workflow and native CMYK print production. If your livelihood sits inside an Adobe pipeline, treat GIMP as a supplement; if you're not in that pipeline, it's a full tool.
Make the interface friendlier
Free Photoshop-style themes and single-window mode soften the landing. Search 'GIMP PhotoGIMP' — a community layout that remaps the interface toward what Photoshop hands expect.

listings.name: VLC Media Player
listings.realcost: Nothing. Run by a nonprofit.
free_includes: Plays virtually every audio and video format ever made
Streams network video and internet radio
Converts files between formats
Subtitle support, no ads, no account
listings.short: Yes — VLC plays essentially any audio or video file ever made, free, with no account, no ads, and no upsell. It is one of the cleanest deals on the internet.
listings.catch: None. Just make sure you download from videolan.org — copycat sites bundle junk with it.
listings.worth: Paid 'player pro' apps charge $5–20 for less than this does free.
smart_moves: Download only from videolan.org — 'VLC Pro' apps in stores are repackagers
It also converts and records video — check the Media menu before buying a converter app
Donations are the only money that should ever change hands here
listings.get_summary: In practice: whatever file anyone sends you, it opens. That's the whole promise, kept for two decades.
drawbacks: Utilitarian interface
No library management like a media-center app
more_info: Who pays for this?

The VideoLAN nonprofit, running on donations. The project has publicly refused advertising and bundling money over the years — which is why, uniquely among 'free players,' it has never shipped a toolbar, an ad, or an upsell.
The copycat problem, explained

Because VLC is famous and free, fake versions are the main risk: 'VLC Pro' listings, lookalike download sites, bundled installers. The real one lives at videolan.org and is free on every platform. If money is requested anywhere, you're not looking at VLC.

listings.name: Blender
listings.realcost: Nothing. Funded by an industry foundation.
free_includes: Modeling, sculpting, rigging, and animation
Two production render engines (Cycles and Eevee)
Video editing and VFX tracking built in
Full commercial use of everything you make
listings.short: Yes. Blender is a full 3D creation suite — modeling, animation, rendering, video editing — used on real studio productions, and every feature is free.
listings.catch: No catch. The 'price' is that 3D itself is hard; expect tutorials, not invoices.
listings.worth: Professional 3D suites run into the thousands per year; Blender charges nothing.
smart_moves: Get it from blender.org and start with the famous free 'donut' beginner tutorial
Everything you make is fully yours to sell — no license anxiety
Ignore paid 'Blender courses' until you've exhausted the vast free ones
listings.get_summary: In practice: a career's worth of 3D software — films, game assets, product renders — with zero licensing cost.
drawbacks: 3D itself is genuinely hard — expect months, not days
Heavy scenes want a strong computer
more_info: Who funds a free professional 3D suite?
The Blender Foundation's Development Fund, backed by studios and companies that rely on it. The industry pays so the tool stays free — a rare model that has held for years and produced feature films.
Is commercial use really unrestricted?

Yes. Blender is open source and everything you create with it is entirely yours — sell renders, games, animations, client work, no license fees and no royalty. The license covers the software, not your output.
The honest cost: time
Blender's price is the learning curve of 3D itself. The community's standard on-ramp is the official beginner ('donut') tutorial series — free, and genuinely the right first step.

listings.name: Audacity
listings.realcost: Nothing. Open source audio editor.
free_includes: Multitrack recording and editing
Noise reduction and a full effects rack
Exports to MP3, WAV, OGG and more
No time limits, no watermarked audio
listings.short: Yes — Audacity records and edits audio with no account, no card, and no export watermark. Podcasters have run on it for two decades.
listings.catch: None that costs money. It's dated-looking and single-purpose, and that's fine.
listings.worth: Paid audio editors run $50–300; this covers podcast and recording basics free.
smart_moves: Official download from audacityteam.org
Add the free FFmpeg plugin to unlock extra import and export formats
It records system audio and mics alike — try it before buying recording software
listings.get_summary: In practice: record a podcast, clean the audio, export it — the entire basic audio workflow, free.
drawbacks: Dated interface
Old-school destructive editing model
Serious music production wants a full DAW instead
more_info: The 2021 privacy flap, in context

After changing hands, Audacity briefly proposed telemetry that angered users; the plan was scaled back after backlash. Current builds keep data collection minimal and optional — check preferences once and move on, or use the community fork Tenacity if you'd rather not think about it.
Formats and the FFmpeg add-on
Out of the box it covers WAV, MP3, and OGG. Installing the free FFmpeg library unlocks nearly everything else (M4A, video-file audio, and more) — a two-minute setup that most users should just do.

listings.name: OBS Studio
listings.realcost: Nothing. The streaming standard, free.
free_includes: Unlimited screen recording and live streaming
Scenes, overlays, and audio mixing
Streams to YouTube, Twitch, and anywhere else
No watermark, no time cap
listings.short: Yes. OBS is the tool most streamers and screen-recorders actually use — full featured, no watermark, no time limit, no account.
listings.catch: No catch. 'OBS-based' paid apps exist that resell what OBS gives away; skip them.
listings.worth: Paid screen recorders charge $100–300 one-time for less.
smart_moves: Download from obsproject.com and run the built-in auto-configuration wizard first
Streamlabs and similar apps are wrappers around this free engine — fine, but know it
No watermark exists — if your recording has one, you're not using real OBS
listings.get_summary: In practice: everything a streamer or course-creator needs to capture and broadcast, at zero cost of entry.
drawbacks: Initial setup has a learning curve
It captures; editing happens elsewhere
more_info: Streamlabs and the wrapper economy
Several polished apps are built on top of OBS's free engine and monetize the convenience layer. They're legitimate, but know what's underneath: the recording and streaming power is the free part.
Getting past the intimidating first screen
Run the built-in Auto-Configuration Wizard on first launch — it tests your machine and picks sane settings. Most 'OBS is complicated' complaints end there.

listings.name: 7-Zip
listings.realcost: Nothing.
free_includes: Opens zip, 7z, rar, tar and most archive formats
High-ratio 7z compression
AES-256 encrypted archives
No nag screens, ever
listings.short: Yes — 7-Zip compresses and extracts every archive format that matters, free, forever. Unlike WinRAR, it doesn't even nag you.
listings.catch: None. This is what 'free' is supposed to mean.
listings.worth: The $29 WinRAR license nobody was ever going to buy anyway.
smart_moves: Get it from 7-zip.org — one small installer, done
It opens .rar files too, so you never need WinRAR
The right-click menu is the whole workflow
listings.get_summary: In practice: every archive you'll ever receive, opened; every folder you send, smaller.
drawbacks: Windows-first — Mac users need an alternative
Spartan interface
more_info: Why is there no catch?
7-Zip is the work of one developer, Igor Pavlov, released under an open license since 1999. There's no company to monetize you — which is why it has out-survived every nagware competitor.
On a Mac?
There's no official Mac version. Keka and The Unarchiver are the free equivalents Mac users reach for; the 7z format itself works everywhere.

listings.name: Canva
listings.limits: Premium templates, elements, and tools locked
listings.realcost: An account, and steady Pro upsells while you work.
free_includes: The full drag-and-drop editor, no watermark on your designs
1.6+ million free templates
4.7+ million free photos, graphics, and elements
5GB of cloud storage
PNG, JPG, and PDF export
paid_locked: Premium (crown-marked) templates and the 140M+ premium library
listings.short: Yes — Canva's free plan is real and permanent: the full editor, over a million templates, millions of free elements, and 5GB of storage, without ever entering a card.
listings.catch: The library is salted with Pro-only templates and elements (marked with a crown), so you'll feel the upsell mid-design — and the background remover, the tool everyone wants, is paid. The free tier still genuinely holds up.
listings.worth: Canva Pro runs $120/year; the free tier covers most casual design work.
smart_moves: Crown icon = paid — back out before investing an hour in a premium template
Verified teachers and registered nonprofits get Pro free — apply if you qualify
Need a transparent PNG? Free external background-removers exist; that's the workaround for the biggest missing tool
listings.get_summary: In practice: real social posts, flyers, decks, and invitations at professional polish — most casual users never need Pro.
drawbacks: Crown-marked premium bait is scattered everywhere
No background remover or transparent export on free
Your designs live in their cloud, not on your disk
more_info: The crown system, decoded

Every template, photo, and element is either free or crown-marked premium. The frustration: search results mix them freely and there's no clean free-only filter, so check for crowns before investing an hour in a design. Occasionally Canva offers single-item purchases as a middle path.
Who owns your designs?

Your designs are yours, but usage rights for stock elements inside them follow Canva's content license — fine for normal business use, with restrictions on things like reselling the elements themselves. For logos and trademarks, read their licensing page before committing.
The free-Pro back doors

Verified K-12 teachers get Canva Pro free through Canva for Education, and registered nonprofits through Canva for Nonprofits. Both are real programs with an application step — if you qualify, this is the whole game.
listings.canada: Available in Canada. Canva for Education verified for Canadian teachers.

listings.name: Notion
listings.realcost: An account. Personal use is close to unlimited.
free_includes: Unlimited pages and blocks for personal use
Sync across all your devices
Databases, templates, and web publishing of pages
Invite a handful of guests
listings.short: Yes — for individuals, Notion's free plan is one of the most generous in software: unlimited pages and blocks for personal workspaces, no card, no clock.
listings.catch: The squeeze is aimed at teams — invite collaborators and block limits plus admin features push you to paid. Solo users can live free indefinitely.
listings.worth: The paid plan runs about $100+/year; solo users get effectively all of it free.
smart_moves: Stay solo in your workspace — inviting teammates is what triggers the block limits
Share pages by public link instead of adding members
Export your data now and then; free shouldn't mean locked in
listings.get_summary: In practice: a complete personal organization system — notes, databases, planning — genuinely unlimited for one person.
drawbacks: Team use hits block limits fast
Offline mode is weak
Its flexibility is its own learning curve
more_info: What a 'block' is and why it's the fence

Everything in Notion — a paragraph, a to-do, a database row — is a block. Personal use is unlimited; the squeeze arrives when a workspace becomes collaborative, where free team trials run into a block cap. That's the precise line between 'free forever' and 'pay up.'
Getting your data out
Notion exports to Markdown, CSV, HTML, and PDF. Do it occasionally: the free tier is generous, but generosity plus deep lock-in is exactly the combination worth insuring against with a backup.
listings.canada: Available in Canada. Education plan works with Canadian .edu equivalents.

listings.name: Figma
listings.limits: 3 shared design files per team, 3 pages per file
listings.realcost: An account, and a team-file cap that working designers hit fast.
free_includes: Unlimited personal draft files
3 shared team design files (plus 3 FigJam and 3 Slides files)
Up to 3 pages per file
Unlimited collaborators can join and edit
30 days of version history
listings.short: Yes — Figma's Starter plan is genuinely free with no card and no expiry: unlimited personal drafts, real-time collaboration, and enough shared files to learn the tool or run one small project.
listings.catch: The wall is precise: a shared team project caps at 3 design files of 3 pages each. Solo practice can stay free forever; team work hits the ceiling almost immediately — which is exactly the point.
listings.worth: A Professional seat runs about $16–20/month; free covers learning and solo work.
smart_moves: Work in personal drafts — those are unlimited; the 3-file cap only hits shared team projects
Hit the cap? Move older files back to drafts to open slots
Students and educators can get the paid tier free through Figma's education program
listings.get_summary: In practice: enough to learn professional design, build a portfolio, and run one live project without paying.
drawbacks: 3 shared files of 3 pages each is a hard team wall
Only 30 days of version history
Needs internet — no real offline mode
more_info: Drafts vs. team files — the loophole spelled out

Personal drafts are unlimited; the famous 3-file cap applies only to files placed in a shared team project. You can move files between drafts and the team space, so solo work never hits a wall — the cap is really a collaboration tax.
Students and teachers ride free
Figma's education program grants the paid tier free with verification. If you're learning design at any institution, apply before paying anything.
The 30-day history window
Free keeps only a month of version history. For long projects, periodically duplicate the file as a manual checkpoint — a free habit that substitutes for the paid feature.
listings.canada: Available in Canada. Education program accepts Canadian institutions.

listings.name: Zoom
listings.realcost: Your meeting dies at minute 40.
free_includes: Unlimited number of meetings
Up to 100 participants
Screen sharing, chat, and reactions
Recording to your own computer
listings.short: Sort of. The free tier is real and permanent — but per Zoom's own support pages, almost all meetings hosted by a free account end at 40 minutes, and that now includes one-on-one calls.
listings.catch: The 40-minute wall exists purely to make you upgrade, and it's harsher than most people remember: 1-on-1 calls used to be unlimited, and they aren't anymore. The free workaround is ending and rejoining with the same link — undignified, but it works.
listings.worth: A paid seat costs real money monthly; free is workable if you play the clock.
smart_moves: Schedule with a reusable link; near minute 38, everyone leaves and rejoins for a fresh 40
The timer starts when the first person joins — don't let guests in early while you're away
If 40 minutes chafes, Google Meet's free tier gives 60 for groups
listings.get_summary: In practice: every meeting under 40 minutes is free forever at full quality — plenty for check-ins and calls.
drawbacks: 40-minute cutoff on ALL meetings — even 1-on-1s now
Cloud recording is paid; recordings save to your machine
The rejoin workaround interrupts every long meeting
more_info: The 40-minute rule, precisely

Per Zoom's own support pages, meetings hosted by a free account cap at 40 minutes — and this now includes 1-on-1 calls, which were once unlimited. The countdown starts when the first participant joins, even if that's before the host, and a warning appears near the end.
Recording: local yes, cloud no
Free accounts can record to their own computer, which covers most needs. Cloud recording, transcripts, and sharing links are paid — plan to upload your local file to Drive if others need it.
If 40 minutes doesn't fit your life
The rejoin trick (same link, fresh timer) works but interrupts. Google Meet's free tier runs 60 minutes for groups — sometimes the smart play is a different room.

free_alternatives.name: Google Drive & Gmail
free_alternatives.why: Google Meet's free tier gives 60-minute group calls

listings.canada: Same free tier worldwide, including the 40-minute cap.

sentiment.split: Trustpilot 1.6/5 from consumers · G2 4.5/5 from business users
sentiment.praised: Business reviewers rate reliability and call quality highly — the product works, which is why it won the market.
sentiment.complained: Consumer complaints concentrate on billing and support: unexpected renewals, difficulty reaching a human, and the 40-minute cap now applying to one-on-one calls that used to be unlimited.
sentiment.takeaway: A near-five-star product with a near-one-star billing experience. Read the split as: the software is fine, watch the subscription.

listings.name: Slack
listings.limits: 90-day visible history; data deleted after 1 year
listings.realcost: Your own chat history, held behind the paid tier.
free_includes: Unlimited members in a workspace
Your most recent 90 days of messages and files
10 app integrations
5GB of shared file storage
1-on-1 huddle calls (30 minutes)
paid_locked: Messages older than 90 days (permanently deleted at 1 year)
listings.short: Sort of. Free Slack works fine day to day — until you search for something from four months ago and find it's been hidden behind the upgrade wall.
listings.catch: Your older messages aren't deleted right away; they're withheld — pay and the last year reappears. But on the free plan anything older than one year is permanently erased, so the archive you think you're keeping is quietly evaporating.
listings.worth: Slack Pro is $7.25 per user per month — your own history is what it sells back.
smart_moves: Export public-channel history regularly — free-plan admins can, and day 91 is too late
Know the hard line: free-plan data older than one year is deleted forever, even if you upgrade later
Keep decisions and specs in a docs tool; let Slack be the chatter layer it's priced as
listings.get_summary: In practice: a fine live chat room for a small team — as long as you treat anything older than a season as gone.
drawbacks: A 90-day visibility wall on your own messages
Free-plan data older than one year is permanently deleted
The 10-app cap pinches real workflows
more_info: Hidden vs. deleted — the exact timeline

Days 1–90: visible. After day 90: hidden but stored — upgrade and the past year reappears. After one year on the free plan: permanently deleted, and no upgrade brings it back. That last rule is the one teams learn too late.
What you can export, and what you can't

Free workspace admins can export public-channel history. Private channels and DMs are excluded — those need paid plans with compliance export. So the 'we'll export before day 90' plan only covers the public half of your conversations.
Calls on free
Huddles are 1-on-1 only and capped around half an hour; group calls are paid. For team calls, free users bolt on Meet or Zoom — awkward, but standard practice.
listings.canada: Same free tier worldwide. Paid plans billed in USD for Canadian teams.

listings.name: Grammarly
listings.realcost: An account, your text on their servers, and constant Premium nudges.
free_includes: Core spelling, grammar, and punctuation checking
Works across your browser, email, and docs
Tone detection basics
listings.short: Sort of. Basic spelling and grammar checking is genuinely free with no card — but the app constantly shows you grayed-out 'advanced' fixes it won't reveal.
listings.catch: Showing you the number of Premium suggestions it's withholding is the business model. Also worth knowing: your writing is processed on their servers.
listings.worth: Premium runs about $144/year; the free layer catches the embarrassing mistakes.
smart_moves: Never paste confidential text — your writing is processed on their servers
The free browser extension already covers email and docs; you don't need more
For rewrites, a free AI chat assistant does what Premium charges $144/year for
listings.get_summary: In practice: it catches the typos and grammar slips that embarrass you — the safety-net layer is genuinely free.
drawbacks: Constant Premium nagging with grayed-out fixes
Your text is processed on their servers
Free suggestions stay surface-level
more_info: Where your text actually goes

Suggestions are computed on Grammarly's servers, which means your prose transits their cloud. For personal writing that's a normal trade; for confidential client or legal material, it's a reason to pause the extension on those tabs.
The withheld-suggestions meter

Free shows you a count of 'advanced suggestions' it found but won't reveal — clarity rewrites, tone shifts, word choice. That number is the sales pitch. A free AI assistant will happily do full rewrites if you paste the text, which quietly deflates the Premium case.

listings.name: Dropbox
listings.realcost: 2GB is a starter apartment; photos alone will evict you.
free_includes: 2GB of synced cloud storage
File sharing links
Sync on up to 3 devices
30 days of file recovery
listings.short: Sort of. The free account is permanent and card-free, but 2GB of storage is deliberately tiny — a fraction of what rivals hand out — and you can only link 3 devices.
listings.catch: 2GB fills in weeks of normal use, and then every sync error becomes an upgrade prompt. Fine as a shared-folder utility; not a real backup.
listings.worth: Dropbox Plus runs about $120/year; free is a 2GB courier, not a backup.
smart_moves: Use it for active shared folders only — Google's free 15GB is seven times bigger for backup
Stay under 3 linked devices or syncing locks you out
When '2GB full' surprises you, empty deleted files and cache first
listings.get_summary: In practice: a reliable pipe for sharing folders with clients. As storage, it's a thimble.
drawbacks: 2GB fills almost immediately
3-device cap on free
Rivals hand out 5–7x the space free
more_info: The 3-device rule
Since 2019, free accounts can sync on at most three linked devices. Add a fourth and you must unlink one first. Phone, laptop, tablet — that's the whole allowance.
What silently eats the 2GB

Camera uploads. If the mobile app is auto-backing-up photos, the tank fills in weeks and every sync error becomes an upgrade prompt. Turn camera upload off and Dropbox goes back to being a fine folder-sharing utility.

free_alternatives.name: Google Drive & Gmail
free_alternatives.why: 15GB free — seven times Dropbox's 2GB, with a full office suite

listings.name: Google Drive & Gmail
listings.realcost: Your usage data feeds Google's ad machine.
free_includes: 15GB of storage shared across Gmail, Drive, and Photos
Full Docs, Sheets, and Slides — real-time collaboration included
Sharing, permissions, and offline access
Apps on every platform
listings.short: Yes — 15GB of storage plus a full office suite, permanently free and genuinely usable. It's the benchmark other free tiers get measured against.
listings.catch: You pay in data: activity across Google services informs the ad business. The 15GB also fills eventually since Gmail and Photos draw from the same tank.
listings.worth: 15GB free next to Dropbox's 2GB tells you who's subsidizing whom — the ad business is buying your attention.
smart_moves: Storage full? Purge Gmail's giant attachments and old Photos first — it's one shared tank
Use Google Takeout to export everything periodically; free shouldn't mean captive
The data trade bothers you? Review the Web & App Activity settings — it's adjustable
listings.get_summary: In practice: a complete free office — real email, 15GB, and a collaborative suite good enough that businesses run on it.
drawbacks: You are the product — usage data feeds the ad business
One 15GB tank drains across three services
Deep lock-in once your life lives inside
more_info: How the ad trade actually touches you

Signals from your activity across Google services inform ad targeting. You can inspect and trim this at myactivity.google.com and the Ads settings — the trade is real but adjustable. (Paid Workspace accounts run under different, stricter terms.)
Managing the shared 15GB tank

Gmail attachments, Drive files, and Photos all draw from one pool. Google's storage manager shows the biggest offenders — usually years of email attachments — and clearing those postpones paying for years.
Leaving is allowed
Google Takeout exports everything — mail, files, photos — in open formats. Free shouldn't mean captive; knowing the exit exists is part of using the free tier wisely.
listings.canada: Identical free tier in Canada. Google One paid storage priced in CAD.

listings.name: Proton VPN
listings.limits: Few server locations, one device, no country choice
listings.realcost: Slower speeds and no country choice on free.
free_includes: Unlimited data — no bandwidth cap
A strict no-logs policy funded by paid tiers, not your data
One device
Auto-connects you to a server from a handful of countries
listings.short: Yes — and it's the rare free VPN worth trusting: no card, no data caps, and a business model (paid tiers) that doesn't involve selling you.
listings.catch: Free users can't pick their server country, get one device, and speeds dip at peak times. That's a fair trade, not a trap.
listings.worth: Trustworthy paid VPNs run $60–100/year; this is the honest free one.
smart_moves: Accept the no-country-choice limit — that's the honest price of a trustworthy free VPN
Ignore 'best free VPN' lists full of data harvesters; the funding model is the only question that matters
Need a specific country or streaming? That's legitimately the paid tier's job
listings.get_summary: In practice: genuine private browsing with no data cap — the rare free VPN that isn't itself the trap.
drawbacks: No choosing your server country
One device only
Speeds dip at peak times
more_info: Why this free tier is trustworthy

Proton is a Swiss company funded by its paid subscriptions across a privacy suite, with an independently audited no-logs policy. The free tier is marketing for the paid one — an honest funding model, unlike free VPNs that monetize the users themselves.
How free connections work
You get one device and an auto-assigned server from a handful of countries — no choosing. Data is unlimited, speeds are decent off-peak. Those limits are the entire price.
listings.canada: Free servers available in Canada — one of the few free VPN countries offered.

listings.name: Hola VPN
listings.realcost: Your device can become part of the product.
free_includes: A browser extension that unblocks some sites
paid_locked: A version that doesn't route strangers' traffic through the network you're part of
listings.short: No — and this is the textbook fake. Hola has historically operated as a peer-to-peer network where free users' connections and bandwidth were routed and resold.
listings.catch: With free VPNs, the honest question is what's being sold, because running servers costs money. Here the answer has, at times, been: you. Use Proton VPN's free tier instead.
listings.worth: Less than free — you may be paying with your connection itself.
smart_moves: Uninstall it and use Proton VPN's free tier instead
Rule of thumb: a free VPN with no visible funding model IS the funding model
listings.get_summary: In practice: it will unblock a site — while historically making your connection part of the product being sold.
drawbacks: Documented history of reselling user bandwidth
Serious unresolved privacy questions
A better free option exists (Proton VPN)
more_info: What 'peer-to-peer VPN' meant in practice

Hola historically routed traffic through its own users and resold that bandwidth through a commercial sister brand. Translated: free users' connections became infrastructure sold to paying customers — with the security implications that carrying strangers' traffic implies.
If you've used it
Uninstall the extension and use Proton VPN's free tier instead. The general rule survives the specific product: with any free VPN, identify who's paying the server bills before you trust it.

free_alternatives.name: Proton VPN
free_alternatives.why: A free VPN funded by paid tiers, not by selling your bandwidth

listings.name: Adobe Creative Cloud
listings.realcost: Auto-converts to a paid plan; early cancellation carries a 50% fee on annual plans.
free_includes: 7 days of full access to the apps in your chosen plan
After cancelling, a free membership keeps limited starter apps and 5GB of files
listings.short: No. Photoshop's 'free trial' runs seven days and rolls straight into a paid subscription unless you cancel first — and the default is usually an annual plan billed monthly.
listings.catch: Adobe's own terms spell out the deeper trap: cancel an annual-billed-monthly plan after the first 14 days and you owe 50% of the remaining year as an early termination fee. That fee structure drew a US federal regulator's lawsuit over how it was disclosed. Set an alarm the day you start the trial.
listings.worth: Played straight, it's one week of $55+/month software. After that, the meter never stops.
smart_moves: If you ever paid Adobe's early termination fee between 2019 and 2025, check the DOJ settlement FAQ — you may be owed compensation
Set an alarm for day 6 — the trial converts silently on day 7
The full-refund window is only the first 14 days; after that the 50% cancellation fee applies to annual plans
Trapped mid-year? Switching to a different (even cheaper) Adobe plan avoids the fee entirely
Photopea in the browser does most of Photoshop, actually free
listings.get_summary: In practice: seven full days of the industry-standard suite — real value if you plan the week and the exit in advance.
drawbacks: Converts silently to a paid annual plan on day 7
50% early-termination fee after day 14 on annual plans
The cancellation design drew a federal lawsuit
more_info: The DOJ settlement — you may be owed money

In March 2026 Adobe settled a US Department of Justice lawsuit over its subscription disclosure and cancellation practices for $150 million: $75 million in free services to affected customers and $75 million paid to the DOJ. Eligibility runs broadly to people who held a Creative Cloud subscription between January 2019 and July 2025 and paid an early termination fee that, combined with their payments, exceeded what a month-to-month plan would have cost. Active subscribers receive free months applied automatically. If you were ever charged Adobe's 50% exit fee, check Adobe's settlement FAQ — this is your money.
The fee math, spelled out

The default subscription is 'annual, billed monthly.' Cancel within 14 days: full refund. Cancel after: you owe 50% of every remaining month in your year. Cancel a $70/month plan six months in, and that's roughly $210 for software you're choosing to stop using.
The regulator's case

US federal regulators sued Adobe over how these fees and the cancellation flow were disclosed — the legal system's way of confirming what canceled users had been saying. Terms have gotten clearer since; the fee structure itself remains, so read your plan type at signup.
The escape hatch Adobe will show you

Mid-cancellation, Adobe offers a plan switch — moving to a different (even cheaper) plan avoids the termination fee entirely, starting a new term instead. Also real: hardship waivers via support chat sometimes succeed. Both beat paying the fee.
Free Adobe that actually exists
Acrobat Reader and the basic tier of Adobe Express are genuinely free. And Photopea — an unrelated in-browser editor — covers most everyday Photoshop work at $0. The trial isn't the only door.

sentiment.split: Trustpilot 1.2/5 from consumers · G2 4.6/5 from professionals
sentiment.praised: Nobody disputes the software. Professionals rate the tools near-perfect on G2 — Photoshop and Premiere remain industry standards, and the review split reflects that: people love the product and resent the contract.
sentiment.complained: Consumer reviews cluster almost entirely on one thing, and it isn't quality — it's the exit. Reviewers describe signing up for what looked like a monthly plan, discovering it was an annual contract, and being charged 50% of the remaining balance to leave. The US Department of Justice made the same finding.
sentiment.takeaway: The gap between a 1.2 and a 4.6 isn't confusion — it's the difference between using Adobe and trying to leave it. That gap is the entire reason this site exists.

You live at a wixsite.com address

more_info: What the banner and subdomain cost you
A business on a wixsite.com address with a Wix ad reads as temporary to customers and to Google. The free tier is genuinely useful — as a build-and-learn sandbox, not a storefront.
The lock-in caveat

Wix sites don't export to other hosts; leaving means rebuilding. Decide whether you're okay marrying the platform before pouring months of content in — or build on plain HTML hosting (see Netlify) where your files are portable forever.

drawbacks: Bandwidth and build allowances are generous but real
The dashboard assumes some technical comfort
more_info: What the free allowances mean for a normal site

A typical small business or portfolio site uses a tiny fraction of the free bandwidth and build allotments. The limits exist for viral moments and heavy apps — if you hit them, your site is succeeding.
Deploying without touching code

netlify.com/drop accepts a dragged folder and publishes it in seconds; updating is dragging again. Add a custom domain in settings and HTTPS is issued automatically, free. This is the no-banner, own-your-files alternative to builder free tiers.

Any repository can publish a static website free — portfolio, documentation, project page — including at your own custom domain. People pay monthly for hosting that does less.
Public-by-default culture

Private repos are free and unlimited, but the platform's defaults and culture lean public. Check the visibility setting at creation, especially before pushing anything with credentials in it — the most common GitHub accident.

more_info: The training toggle
By default, consumer chats may be used to improve models. Settings → Data Controls turns this off, and Temporary Chats skip history entirely. Flip it once if your prompts touch anything sensitive.
Why free feels smarter at 7am
Free users are first to be throttled when demand spikes: slower responses, fallback to lesser models, tighter caps. Off-peak hours quietly return the premium experience without the subscription.

more_info: How the daily allowance behaves

Caps reset on a rolling basis and long inputs cost more than short ones — pasting a whole book into one message spends the day's budget fast. Fewer, richer messages stretch further than rapid-fire fragments.
What paid actually buys
Higher caps, priority at peak, and the heaviest models and features. The free tier isn't a demo — it's the same assistant with a meter — so the upgrade question is purely about volume.

Bundled convenience inside Gmail and Docs is the free tier's real gift. The frontier-model heavy lifting sits in the paid tier — for hard problems, comparing answers across the free assistants costs nothing.

Sources are the product's honesty mechanism, but they can be thin or misread. Click through on anything that matters — the link list is an invitation to verify, not a substitute for it.

Models carry individual licenses — some fully open, some research-only, some restricted commercially. The license tag on each model page is the two-second check that prevents a legal headache later.

Khan Academy is a nonprofit funded by donations and foundations. No ads, no premium curriculum, no data resale — the free-ness is the mission, which is why it's the benchmark this site judges 'free learning' against.

Khanmigo, the AI tutor, is a paid add-on — the single exception on the platform. Everything else, including all courses, practice, and dashboards, remains fully free.

The model
A donor-supported nonprofit. No upsell exists anywhere in the curriculum, which is why finishing Codecademy's free tier and 'graduating' here is this site's standing advice.

The workload warning
CS50 is famously demanding — problem sets take real hours. That's the value: it's free in dollars, not in effort, and the effort is exactly what makes it worth listing.

listings.short: Less than it used to be. The course content is still free — but in 2025 Duolingo replaced hearts with an 'energy' meter on mobile that drains with every exercise, right or wrong, walling most free users off after a couple of lessons a day.
listings.catch: Under hearts, a careful learner could study free all day; under energy, even perfect answers burn the meter, and refilling means watching ads, spending gems, waiting, or subscribing. The content is free. The time to actually learn it, on mobile, increasingly isn't — which is why we moved Duolingo down a tier.

more_info: The energy system, precisely

Rolled out from 2025 on mobile: a meter (around 25 units) that drains with every exercise — including correct answers — replacing hearts, which only punished mistakes. Refills come from ads, gems, waiting, or streak bonuses, and the rollout is staggered, so two phones can behave differently.
The web loophole

As of our check, the browser version still runs the older hearts system: mistakes cost, perfection doesn't. Free users serious about volume should simply study at duolingo.com — no promise it lasts, but it's real today.
Is free still worth it?

The content — full courses in every language — remains free and identical to paid. What changed is daily time: free mobile users hit the wall after a couple of lessons. As a daily habit-builder it still works; as an intensive tool, free no longer suffices on mobile.

sentiment.split: Trustpilot 2.2/5 from a large, vocal user base
sentiment.praised: Nobody disputes that the free tier teaches, or that the streak mechanic works — millions keep coming back, which is itself the review.
sentiment.complained: The energy meter dominates recent complaints. Long-time users describe a system that now drains even on correct answers, capping free mobile learning at a couple of lessons a day where it once ran unlimited.
sentiment.takeaway: The low score isn't people saying the app doesn't work. It's people saying it used to be better before the free tier was throttled toward a subscription.

Open the course page, click Enroll, and look below the payment options for a small 'Audit the course' text link. It grants full lectures and readings, no card. Not every course offers it — specializations and some professional certificates don't.
Financial aid is real, not decorative

Most courses show 'Financial aid available' by the enroll button. The application takes a short essay and about two weeks, and approval unlocks the complete paid course — graded work and certificate included — free. Slow, legitimate, underused.

listings.catch: Free Codecademy teaches you enough to want the rest. If you finish the free track, freeCodeCamp will take you further for nothing.
listings.worth: Pro runs hundreds a year; the free basics are a decent on-ramp.
smart_moves: Finish a free course, then move to freeCodeCamp — deeper, and still $0
Ignore the countdown-timer sales; they recur forever
The free tier's real value is discovering whether you like coding at all
listings.get_summary: In practice: a gentle, hand-held first month of coding — the free tier answers 'is this for me?'
drawbacks: Paths, projects, and certificates are all paid
Free content runs shallow past the basics
Perpetual countdown-sale pressure
more_info: What free actually contains
A real set of beginner courses — Python, JavaScript, HTML/CSS among them — in a friendly in-browser editor, plus free documentation. The intermediate ramp, projects, and certificates are Pro.
The eternal countdown sale
Discount timers recur perpetually; urgency is the design, not the reality. If you outgrow free, compare against freeCodeCamp (deeper, $0) before any sale convinces you.

listings.name: Skillshare
listings.realcost: Auto-converts to a paid annual or monthly membership.
free_includes: Nothing without entering a card — the 'free' is a trial of the full library
paid_locked: The entire class library, once the trial lapses
listings.short: No. Skillshare's 'free trial' wants your card at the door and converts to a paid membership automatically when the trial lapses.
listings.catch: The classic pattern: generous-sounding trial, card required, and the billing date does the selling. Cancel the same day you sign up — the trial keeps running either way.
listings.worth: Played straight: a month of classes free. Played asleep: a $100+/year charge.
smart_moves: Set a cancellation alarm three days before the trial ends
Cancel on the website, not the app, and screenshot the confirmation
Many teachers post sample classes free on YouTube — audition before trialing
listings.get_summary: In practice: one month of the full class library — real value only if the exit is already on your calendar.
drawbacks: Card required before anything plays
Auto-bills the moment the trial ends
Class quality varies wildly
more_info: How the trial actually behaves

Card first, then a trial whose length varies by promotion, then automatic billing — often for an annual plan, which stings hardest. Cancelling typically leaves access running through the trial's end, so the safest pattern is subscribe, calendar it, cancel early.
Try before trialing
Many Skillshare teachers publish sample classes on YouTube. Auditioning a teacher there costs nothing and no card — often enough to know whether the library is worth even a well-managed trial.

Certificates earned during the month post to your LinkedIn profile and remain after cancellation. Alarm at day 25, harvest the certificates, decide with a clear head.

listings.name: YouTube
listings.realcost: Your attention, sold to advertisers, and your watch data.
free_includes: The largest video library ever assembled, no account needed
Uploading and running your own channel
Live streams, music, and full-length free movies with ads
paid_locked: Background play and downloads on mobile
listings.short: Yes — the largest video library ever assembled is free to watch with no account at all. Ads are the toll, and Premium exists to remove them.
listings.catch: Free means ad-funded: you pay in interruptions and in a detailed profile of what you watch. As pure entertainment value per dollar, it's still unbeatable.
listings.worth: Premium is about $14/month; free is the same library with tolls.
smart_moves: No account is needed to watch — sign in only when you want subscriptions and history

For children, YouTube Kids is the free, contained version

drawbacks: Ads, increasingly frequent
Your watch data builds a profile
The algorithm is engineered to keep you longer than you planned
more_info: The free-movies shelf people miss
Under Movies & TV, YouTube runs a legitimate section of full, licensed, ad-supported films — free. Worth a look before paying to rent the same title one menu over.
Taming the data trade
Watch and search history power both recommendations and ads; both are pausable in your Google account settings. The service works fine with them off — the algorithm just gets less clingy.
For kids
YouTube Kids is the free, contained version with parental controls. Main YouTube's autoplay-and-algorithm design is not built for unsupervised children, free or not.

listings.name: Tubi
listings.realcost: Ad breaks. No card exists anywhere in the product.
free_includes: Thousands of licensed movies and shows
Live channels and a kids section
Watching without creating an account
listings.short: Yes — Tubi streams thousands of real movies and shows free with ads, and there's no premium tier upselling you. You can watch without even making an account.
listings.catch: The catalog is licensed leftovers plus a surprising number of gems — ad-funded TV, honestly labeled. There is nothing to cancel because there is nothing to buy.
listings.worth: Paid streamers run $8–25/month; Tubi is a real catalog at $0.
smart_moves: Skip account creation entirely unless you want a watchlist
Check Tubi before paying to rent a movie — the overlap surprises people
The kids section is genuinely curated and free
listings.get_summary: In practice: a real movie night, legally free — thousands of films including genuine gems, with zero account friction.
drawbacks: Ad breaks
The catalog rotates — favorites leave
Back-catalog heavy; don't expect new releases
more_info: Why is Fox giving away movies?
Tubi is Fox's ad-supported streamer: your attention during ad breaks is the revenue. That's the entire model — no tiers, no card field anywhere, nothing to cancel.
The rotating catalog
Licensing windows mean titles arrive and leave monthly. If something matters to you, watch it this month — 'saved for later' has a shelf life here.

listings.realcost: Ad breaks, same as broadcast TV.
free_includes: Hundreds of free live channels
On-demand movies and shows
No account, no card, ever
listings.short: Yes — hundreds of free live channels plus on-demand titles, no account, no card, no trial. It recreates cable's channel-surfing feel, minus the bill.
listings.catch: None hiding. It's television's old bargain — free programming, paid for by commercials — moved onto the internet.
listings.worth: Cable's channel-surfing, minus the $60+/month cable bill.
smart_moves: Nothing to sign up for — open it and watch
The on-demand library hides behind the live guide; don't miss it
Install it on the TV itself for the full channel-surf experience
listings.get_summary: In practice: instant channel-surfing television — news, movies, nostalgia — free the way broadcast always was.
drawbacks: Ads, like cable
Channel-first layout makes finding a specific title clunky
more_info: The FAST model, explained
Pluto is Paramount's free ad-supported streaming TV — linear channels rebuilt on the internet, funded exactly like broadcast always was. Free isn't a promotion; it IS the business.
Finding the hidden on-demand
The channel guide is the front door, but a full on-demand library of movies and shows sits one tab over. Most casual users never notice it.

listings.name: Spotify
listings.realcost: Ads, and on your phone, control over what plays next.
free_includes: The full music catalog, with ads
Playlists and discovery features
Mostly on-demand listening on desktop
paid_locked: Ad-free playback and top audio quality
listings.short: Yes — the free tier is permanent and card-free with the full music catalog. On desktop it's close to the real product; on mobile it's shuffle-heavy by design.
listings.catch: Mobile free users mostly can't pick the exact song — the single most-wanted feature is the paywall. That one withheld button funds the business.
listings.worth: Premium is about $12/month; free is the whole catalog with handcuffs on mobile.
smart_moves: Do your real listening on desktop — free there is close to full on-demand
On free mobile, podcasts play on-demand; music is the restricted half
If free chafes, the student discount is the cheapest legitimate door
listings.get_summary: In practice: the entire music catalog free — nearly full-featured on desktop, a smart radio on your phone.
drawbacks: Mobile is mostly shuffle with limited skips
Ads between songs
No downloads or offline listening
more_info: Desktop free vs. mobile free — the exact split

On desktop, free users can mostly pick and play songs on demand. On mobile, free means shuffle-dominated playback with limited skips — the platform's most-wanted button, held for ransom. Podcasts, notably, play on-demand everywhere free.
Why it's built this way

Mobile is where listening lives, so mobile is where the squeeze goes. Knowing the design is deliberate helps: do real listening sessions on desktop, treat the phone as radio, and free Spotify stays genuinely pleasant.
listings.canada: Available in Canada with the same free tier. Student discount verified through SheerID with Canadian institutions.

listings.short: Not anymore. Crunchyroll shut down its free ad-supported tier on December 31, 2025; since January 1, 2026, watching anything on the platform requires a paid subscription.
listings.catch: For years the free-with-ads tier was the legal way to watch anime on a budget, and it eroded gradually before being switched off entirely. Some older Crunchyroll titles still surface on genuinely free services like Tubi and Pluto TV — but the platform itself has left the free internet.
listings.worth: $0 — the free tier is gone. Plans start at $7.99/month.
smart_moves: Old Crunchyroll shows stream free on Tubi, Pluto TV, and similar services — look there first
RetroCrush is a free, legal anime service worth knowing
If you do subscribe, the base Fan tier covers most normal viewing

drawbacks: The free tier was fully discontinued
Card and auto-renew on every plan
The free leftovers are scattered across other services
more_info: Timeline of the shutdown

Users saw an in-player notice in late 2025: ad-supported streaming would end December 31, 2025. Since January 1, 2026, a paid subscription is required to watch anything on the platform. Paid tier prices were left unchanged at the switch.
Where the free anime went

Older Crunchyroll titles surface on free ad-supported services — Pluto TV, Tubi, and similar channels — without new episodes or simulcasts. RetroCrush remains a dedicated free, legal anime service. The free option didn't vanish from the world; it left this platform.

Your library grants a monthly credit allowance; most films cost a few credits to start a multi-day viewing window. Credits reset on the 1st — the rhythm is 'a few good films a month,' not a binge service.
Two catalogs in one

Kanopy Kids often plays without consuming credits (library-dependent), making it a genuinely unlimited children's shelf. And participation varies: if your library lacks Kanopy, a second library card somewhere that has it solves everything.

Comics and manga readers: this is the sleeper free source

Library participation varies
The interface is functional, not slick

Hoopla charges libraries per borrow instead of per licensed copy — so everyone can borrow the same title simultaneously. The trade-off is your library caps monthly borrows to control its bill. Use Hoopla for instant needs, Libby for the deep catalog.
The quiet strengths
Comics, manga, and music are unusually strong here — categories other free services barely touch. Everything returns itself; late fees structurally cannot exist.

smart_moves: Calendar day 27 the moment the trial starts

more_info: The cancel-now trick, verified

Start the trial, then immediately cancel: Amazon keeps the benefits active through the full 30 days and simply doesn't bill at the end. It converts the trap into an honest free month — the single best move on this page.
The second toll booth
Since 2024, even paying Prime members see ads unless they add an ad-free upcharge. Factor that into whether the post-trial membership is worth it — the sticker price isn't the ad-free price.

listings.name: Project Gutenberg
listings.realcost: Nothing. Volunteer-run since 1971.
free_includes: Over 70,000 classic books
Every useful format: epub, Kindle, plain text, web
No account, no ads, no limits
Free audiobook versions of many titles
listings.short: Yes — over 70,000 classic books, free to download in every format, no account, no card, no ads. The oldest free library on the internet.
listings.catch: The collection is public domain, so it's the classics — roughly pre-1930 — rather than this year's bestsellers. For those, see Libby.
listings.worth: 70,000+ books at paperback prices would be a small fortune; here it's $0.
smart_moves: Download epub or use send-to-device — no app or account needed
Free audiobook versions exist for many titles; look for the audio link
Want the same book, prettier? Check Standard Ebooks
listings.get_summary: In practice: every classic you were ever assigned and a lifetime more — instantly, in any format, forever.
drawbacks: Nothing modern — public domain only
Plain, utilitarian editions
more_info: Why the cutoff is ~1930 and moving
US copyright expires 95 years after publication, so each January another year of books enters the public domain and Gutenberg's shelf grows. 'The classics' here is a frontier that advances annually.
Reading on your device
Every book downloads as epub, Kindle format, or plain text — and many have free audiobook versions linked. No app, account, or reader lock-in; the files are simply yours.

Formats for every reader: epub, azw3, kepub
Cover art and typography a paid publisher would charge for

listings.catch: None. Smaller catalog than Gutenberg because every title is hand-finished — quality over quantity, at the same price of zero.
listings.worth: Publisher-quality classic editions — the $10–15 paperback, typeset better, free.
smart_moves: Grab the file format matched to your reader — epub, azw3, or kepub
Their new-releases feed doubles as a free classics book club
If it becomes a habit, donate — that's the entire business model

drawbacks: A smaller, curated catalog

more_info: Same texts as Gutenberg — so why come here?

Volunteers take public-domain texts and give them professional typography, careful proofreading, consistent formatting, and original covers. It's the difference between a photocopy and a published edition, at the same price of zero.
Format matching
Grab epub for most readers, azw3 for Kindle, kepub for Kobo — the download page labels them plainly. Their new-releases feed doubles as a free classics book club.

listings.name: Libby
listings.realcost: Nothing — your taxes already bought these books.
free_includes: Current bestsellers as ebooks and audiobooks

Multiple library cards in one app

listings.catch: Popular new releases have waitlists, sometimes long ones. That's the only cost of a service that would otherwise run hundreds of dollars a year.
listings.worth: Two audiobooks a month is $30–50 retail — $400+/year in value on one library card.
smart_moves: Add every library card you're entitled to — cards stack inside one app
Place holds early and use 'deliver later' to pause them until you're ready
In the US, Libby sends library ebooks straight to your Kindle
listings.get_summary: In practice: this year's bestsellers, read or narrated, delivered to your phone or Kindle — your taxes already paid for them.
drawbacks: Waitlists on hot titles
Loans expire on schedule
The catalog depends on your library's budget
more_info: Why bestsellers have waitlists

Publishers license ebooks to libraries copy-by-copy, so your library owns a finite number of each title. The waitlist is the queue for those copies — frustrating, but it's the mechanism that keeps this legal and free.
Stacking library cards

Libby holds multiple cards in one app, and every card multiplies your catalog and shortens effective waits. Many people qualify for more cards than they hold — home, work city, and some libraries offer non-resident cards.
Kindle delivery (US)
In the US, Libby can send borrowed ebooks straight to a Kindle. Borrowed audiobooks play in the Libby app itself everywhere.
listings.canada: Works with Canadian public libraries. Toronto, Vancouver, and most major systems participate.

free_includes: Borrow scanned books with a free account
Read directly in the browser

listings.catch: Lending works like a physical library: copies are limited and loans expire. The catalog has thinned in places, but it remains a genuine free resource.

drawbacks: Short, timed loans
One digital copy per reader at a time
Scan quality varies
more_info: Controlled digital lending, explained

The Internet Archive lends scans of physical books it owns, one digital reader per owned copy — a library model transplanted online. Court rulings have trimmed parts of the catalog, but vast holdings remain, especially the out-of-print.
The two loan types
Popular items lend for an hour at a time (auto-renewing while you read if no one waits); others lend for two weeks. It feels odd for a minute and then just works.

Builders: the API and data are free too

So much depth it takes a little orientation
more_info: How deep it goes

From Tanach and Talmud through Midrash, halacha, and Chassidus, with commentaries interlinked so a verse opens onto everything ever said about it. The connections between texts — not just the texts — are the real gift.
Free beyond the website
The mobile apps work offline once texts are downloaded, and the entire database and API are open for builders. A nonprofit funded by donors; there is nothing to buy.

more_info: Credit mechanics — the part that favors you

The trial includes a credit good for any audiobook regardless of price, and credit-purchased books remain in your library permanently after cancellation. The included 'Plus catalog' is different: it's streaming access that vanishes when you leave.
Cancelling is a negotiation

Attempting to cancel triggers retention offers — pause the membership, or continue at a discount. These are genuine offers; take one knowingly or decline cleanly. Just don't mistake the flow for a wall: cancellation completes if you continue through it.

Pair it with AnnualCreditReport.com for the full official reports
listings.get_summary: In practice: always-on credit visibility that paid services charge for — funded by showing you offers.
drawbacks: Every recommendation is a paid placement
VantageScore shown; most lenders pull FICO
Requires full identity linkage
more_info: VantageScore vs. FICO, explained

Credit Karma shows VantageScore from two bureaus; most lenders pull some version of FICO. The two usually move together but can differ by real margins — so treat Karma's number as a trend line, not the number a mortgage officer will see.
How the free gets paid for

Karma earns bounties when you're approved for products it recommends, which is why recommendations are prominent and personalized. The monitoring is genuinely useful; the 'offers' tab is a shopping mall wearing an advisor's coat.
listings.canada: Available in Canada with Equifax and TransUnion scores. Slightly different feature set than US version.

listings.name: Wave Accounting
listings.limits: Payments, payroll, and some conveniences cost extra
listings.realcost: Nothing for core bookkeeping; they earn on payment processing.
free_includes: Unlimited invoicing with online-payment buttons
Real double-entry accounting and reports
Unlimited collaborators and businesses
paid_locked: Payment processing (per-transaction fees when clients pay online)
Pro conveniences like receipt capture and bank-feed extras
listings.short: Yes — real double-entry accounting and unlimited invoicing for small businesses, free with no card. Wave makes its money when you process payments or run payroll through it.
listings.catch: The free core is genuinely complete, though some conveniences have migrated to a paid Pro tier over the years. The invoice 'pay online' button is the revenue engine — processing fees apply when clients pay through it.
listings.worth: QuickBooks starts around $30+/month; Wave's core books are $0.
smart_moves: Bookkeeping and invoicing stay free — the fees live in payment processing and payroll
Decide consciously whether the invoice 'pay online' button is worth its per-transaction cut
Reconcile monthly; free software still needs paid-level discipline
listings.get_summary: In practice: real books for a small business — invoices out, accounting straight — at a software price of zero.
drawbacks: Processing fees when clients pay invoices online
Payroll costs extra
Some conveniences migrated to a paid Pro tier
more_info: Where Wave makes its money

Payment processing (a per-transaction cut when clients pay invoices online) and payroll subscriptions. The free books are the funnel into those — an honest model, but it means the 'pay online' button on your invoices is their revenue decision disguised as your convenience.
The slow migration to Pro

Over the years some conveniences (like receipt scanning) moved from free to a paid Pro tier. The core — unlimited invoicing and real double-entry accounting — remains free; just expect the perimeter to keep shifting.

listings.name: Wealthsimple
listings.limits: Premium tiers exist; FX fees on USD trades
listings.realcost: Commission-free trading; currency conversion is where fees hide.
free_includes: Commission-free Canadian stock and ETF trades
No account minimums
Registered accounts (TFSA, RRSP) at no fee
Fractional shares
paid_locked: USD accounts that skip the currency-conversion fee
listings.short: Yes — for Canadians, buying and selling stocks is genuinely commission-free with no minimums and no card needed to start.
listings.catch: 'Commission-free' isn't 'fee-free': converting CAD to USD to buy American stocks carries a currency exchange fee unless you pay for a premium tier. That's the quiet toll.
listings.worth: Old-school brokers charged $5–10 per trade; buying Canadian stocks here is $0.
smart_moves: Trade Canadian tickers freely — the currency-conversion fee lives on USD trades
Buying US stocks often? Price the premium tier against what you're losing to FX
The registered accounts (TFSA, RRSP) cost nothing extra here
listings.get_summary: In practice: investing without the toll booth — Canadian trades cost nothing and minimums don't exist.
drawbacks: A currency-conversion fee on USD trades
Research tools are basic
Canada only
more_info: The FX toll, explained

Buying US stocks means converting CAD to USD, and the conversion carries a fee each way on the basic tier. Trade US names often and those slices exceed what commissions ever were — premium tiers sell USD accounts precisely to end this. Canadian tickers carry no such toll.
What protection you have

Wealthsimple's brokerage is a regulated Canadian dealer with standard investor protections on accounts. Commission-free doesn't mean informal — the free tier sits inside the same regulatory frame as the banks' brokerages.
listings.canada: Canada only — this is the home market. Commission-free CAD trades; FX fees on USD.

listings.name: Robinhood
listings.realcost: Your order flow is sold to market makers.
free_includes: Commission-free stock, ETF, and options trades
No account minimum
Basic research and real-time quotes
paid_locked: Gold: margin access, better rates, deeper research
listings.short: Yes — trades are genuinely commission-free with no card. The revenue comes from routing your orders to trading firms that pay for them.
listings.catch: Payment for order flow means the product is free because your trades themselves are the merchandise. Fine to know; just understand what's funding the zero.
listings.worth: Commission-free is table stakes now — this 'free' is funded by your order flow.
smart_moves: Use limit orders — they give you price control that market orders don't
The app is engineered for frequent trading; set your own pace, not its
Gold's margin is borrowed money — understand that before touching its free trial
listings.get_summary: In practice: the zero-commission account that forced a whole industry to zero — the trading itself is genuinely free.
drawbacks: Your order flow is the product
The interface nudges frequent trading
US only
more_info: Payment for order flow, in plain words

Robinhood routes your orders to trading firms that pay for the privilege of executing them. You pay no commission; the firms profit on the spread. Whether execution quality costs you fractions of a cent per share is the long-running debate — 'free' here means the cost moved somewhere harder to see.
The Gold nudge

Gold's margin is borrowed money with interest, wrapped in a friendly trial. There's nothing wrong with margin as a tool — there's plenty wrong with meeting it via a free-trial button. Understand it independently before touching it.

listings.name: AnnualCreditReport.com
listings.realcost: Nothing. Free by federal law.
free_includes: Full credit reports from all three bureaus
Weekly access, mandated by US federal law
The official channel — no enrollment, no trial
listings.short: Yes — this is the one US government-mandated site for genuinely free credit reports from all three bureaus, available weekly. No card, no trial, no upsell.
listings.catch: Two things to know: it provides reports, not scores (Credit Karma covers scores free), and the catch is the imitators — lookalike 'free credit report' sites funnel you into paid monitoring. This exact address is the real one.
listings.worth: The bureaus sell these same reports; federal law makes them free here, weekly.
smart_moves: Type the address directly — lookalike 'free credit report' sites are the actual trap
Stagger the three bureaus across the year for rolling coverage
Remember it's reports, not scores — Credit Karma covers scores free
listings.get_summary: In practice: your complete official credit files, weekly, by law — the most under-used free thing in American finance.
drawbacks: Reports only — no scores
US only
The identity-verification quiz can be finicky
more_info: Why this exists at all

A federal law (the FACT Act) mandates free credit reports, and weekly access — expanded during the pandemic — was made permanent. This site is the three bureaus' joint compliance with that law: the rare 'free' backed by statute rather than a business model.
Reports vs. scores, and the imitators

You receive full reports (accounts, inquiries, errors) — not scores; pair with Credit Karma for free score tracking. And type the address directly: an entire industry of lookalike 'free credit report' sites exists to intercept this exact intention and sell monitoring.
listings.canada: US only. Canadians: request free credit reports directly from Equifax Canada and TransUnion Canada once per year.

listings.name: Rocket Money
listings.limits: Best features behind Premium; negotiation takes a cut
listings.realcost: Premium is 'pay what you want' with a floor; bill negotiation keeps a share of savings.
free_includes: A subscription finder that scans linked accounts
Spending tracking and balance alerts
Basic budgets

listings.short: Sort of. The free app tracks spending and finds subscriptions — but the headline features (cancellation concierge, bill negotiation) are paid, and negotiation keeps a percentage of what it saves you.
listings.catch: An app famous for cancelling unwanted subscriptions is itself a subscription with an upsell flow. The irony is the review.
listings.worth: The free layer is a decent subscription-finder; the famous features are paid.
smart_moves: Use the free tier to FIND subscriptions, then cancel them yourself — that's exactly what the paid concierge does
Bill negotiation keeps a percentage of your savings — read the number before agreeing
The free alternative to all of it: read one card statement line by line, once
