smart_moves: Try Ubuntu from a USB drive before installing — no commitment needed
Install alongside Windows (dual boot) if you're not ready to switch fully
For old hardware, try Linux Mint or Lubuntu — even lighter
more_info: Why an entire operating system is free

Linux is maintained by thousands of contributors, companies, and a global community. Ubuntu's sponsor Canonical earns from enterprise services, not desktop licenses. The model has sustained a free operating system for two decades — and it runs most of the world's servers, phones (Android is Linux), and supercomputers.

listings.name: Cloudflare
listings.limits: Generous free tier; enterprise features paid
listings.realcost: An account. The free tier protects and accelerates real production websites.
listings.short: Yes — Cloudflare's free plan gives real websites a global CDN, DDoS protection, free SSL, DNS hosting, and basic security — the same infrastructure enterprises pay for, at a permanently free tier that's stunningly generous.
listings.catch: Cloudflare's free tier is its market-entry strategy: once your site runs through their network, upgrading for advanced features is frictionless. The free tier is genuinely production-grade.
free_includes: Global CDN — your site served from the nearest of 300+ data centers
Free SSL/TLS certificates, auto-renewing
DDoS attack protection
DNS hosting with one of the fastest resolvers on earth
Basic firewall rules and caching
drawbacks: Configuration has a learning curve
You're routing all traffic through a third party
The dashboard can overwhelm newcomers
listings.get_summary: In practice: professional-grade website security and speed that agencies charge hundreds per month to configure — free for any site, indefinitely.
listings.worth: Comparable CDN and security services run $20–200+/month; Cloudflare's free tier covers most small and medium sites entirely.
smart_moves: Point your domain's nameservers to Cloudflare — the setup wizard walks you through it
Turn on 'Always Use HTTPS' and 'Auto Minify' in the dashboard immediately
The free tier is production-ready — don't pay until you specifically need a paid feature
more_info: Why Cloudflare gives away enterprise infrastructure

Every free site on Cloudflare's network makes the network smarter — more data on attack patterns, more cached content, more routing intelligence. The free tier is the training data for the paid security products. You get real protection; they get scale. Both sides benefit.

listings.name: Canva Docs
listings.realcost: An account. A visual document editor inside Canva's free plan.
listings.short: Yes — Canva Docs is a visual-first document editor included in Canva's free tier: drag in graphics, charts, and media alongside text, then share or export. No card, no separate subscription.
listings.catch: Same model as Canva's design tools — crown-marked premium elements mixed in, background remover and brand kit locked to Pro. The core editor and collaboration are free.
free_includes: A visual document editor with rich media support
Collaboration and sharing
Export to PDF
Part of the existing Canva free plan
drawbacks: Crown-marked premium content mixed in
Dependent on Canva's ecosystem
Less powerful than Google Docs for long-form text
listings.get_summary: In practice: the prettiest way to make a one-pager, proposal, or visual report — free if you dodge the crowns.
listings.worth: Part of the Canva free plan that replaces ~$120/year of Pro for casual users.
smart_moves: Great for proposals and visual one-pagers; less suited for 50-page documents
Same crown-avoidance rules as Canva's main editor apply
Export to PDF for sharing outside Canva

listings.name: Anchor (Spotify for Podcasters)
listings.realcost: Nothing. Spotify wants podcasts on its platform.
listings.short: Yes — Spotify for Podcasters (formerly Anchor) lets you record, edit, host, and distribute a podcast to all major platforms — free, with no hosting fees, no episode limits, and no ads unless you opt in.
listings.catch: Spotify funds this to fill its platform with podcasts. Your show is distributed everywhere (Apple, Google, etc.), not locked to Spotify. The trade is that Spotify gets first-party data on your listeners.
free_includes: Free podcast hosting with no episode or storage limits
Recording and basic editing tools built in
Distribution to Spotify, Apple Podcasts, Google, and more
Optional monetization through ads and subscriptions
drawbacks: Analytics emphasize Spotify metrics over other platforms
Spotify's terms give it broad content rights
Moving to another host later requires redirecting your RSS feed
listings.get_summary: In practice: a complete podcast studio and hosting platform at $0 — the reason the barrier to starting a podcast is now just a microphone and an idea.
listings.worth: Podcast hosting services run $5–20+/month; this covers the full workflow free.
smart_moves: Read the terms on content rights before publishing anything exclusive
Download your own episodes as backups
If you outgrow it, export your RSS feed to another host — it's portable
more_info: Why Spotify gives away podcast hosting

Every podcast hosted on Spotify's platform is content that drives app opens, listening hours, and ad inventory. Free hosting isn't charity — it's a content acquisition strategy that turned Spotify into the world's largest podcast platform. Your show is the product; the hosting is the customer acquisition cost.

listings.name: Prolific
listings.realcost: Nothing. You are paid, never charged.
listings.short: Yes — Prolific pays you to participate in academic and market research studies. Typical pay is $8–15/hour equivalent, studies take 5–30 minutes, and payment goes to PayPal. The platform is used by universities worldwide and is the gold standard of paid research.
listings.catch: Study availability depends on your demographic profile — some people get several a day, others a few per week. It's side income, not a salary. But every penny is real and nobody asks for yours first.
free_includes: Paid research studies from universities and companies
Clear pay rate shown before you accept
PayPal cashout at a low minimum threshold
No fees, no subscriptions, no 'points' — real money
drawbacks: Study flow is unpredictable — some days nothing, some days several
Earnings are supplemental, not full-time income
Must answer screening questions honestly — demographics determine availability
listings.get_summary: In practice: THEY PAY YOU — real money to PayPal for answering research studies honestly. $8–15/hour equivalent, 5–30 minutes per study, cash out from about $5.
listings.worth: You get paid $8–15/hour equivalent, per study, to your PayPal. Realistic monthly total: $50–200 with regular participation.
smart_moves: Fill out your profile completely — every unanswered question is a study you won't see
Install the browser extension to catch studies before they fill
Never rush or lie on screeners — Prolific bans for low-quality responses
Cash out regularly; don't let earnings sit
more_info: Why researchers pay you

Academic studies need participants; recruiting on campus is slow and biased. Prolific gives researchers a diverse, global pool of pre-screened people, and pays fairly because ethical review boards require it. Your data goes into published research — you're compensated, never exploited.
How it compares to survey sites

Most survey sites (Swagbucks, Survey Junkie) pay pennies per hour in points that convert poorly. Prolific pays in real currency at rates reviewed for fairness. It's a different category entirely — research compensation, not attention harvesting.

legitimacy.company: Prolific Academic Ltd — UK company, founded 2014 at Oxford
legitimacy.track_record: Used by researchers at Oxford, Stanford, Google, and thousands of universities; 15,000+ Trustpilot reviews at 4.5/5
legitimacy.red_flags: None structural. Individual complaints center on account bans and study availability, not missing payments.

sentiment.praised: Reviewers consistently confirm the thing that matters most on a paid-research platform: the money arrives, at the rate promised, without games. Fair pay rates and quick PayPal cashouts recur throughout.
sentiment.complained: The friction is availability and account bans — participants describe quiet weeks with no matching studies, and occasional bans they consider unexplained. Notably absent from the complaints: unpaid work.
sentiment.takeaway: When 15,000 reviewers complain about how *few* studies they get rather than whether they were paid, that's the strongest possible signal of legitimacy.

listings.name: UserTesting
listings.limits: Tests offered based on your profile
listings.realcost: Nothing. Companies pay you to test their websites and apps.
listings.short: Yes — companies pay you $4–10+ per test to use their website or app while talking through your experience aloud. Tests take 10–20 minutes. Payment via PayPal, 7 days after the test.
listings.catch: Test frequency depends on matching your demographics to client needs. Some weeks are busy, others quiet. The pay is real; the volume is unpredictable.
free_includes: Paid website and app testing sessions
Screen recording + thinking-aloud format
PayPal payment per completed test
No equipment beyond a computer and microphone
drawbacks: Test availability varies widely
You need a quiet space and working microphone
Payment takes 7 days per test
Screening tests filter you out of many opportunities
listings.get_summary: In practice: THEY PAY YOU — $4 to $10+ per website test, each taking 10–20 minutes. Money arrives by PayPal a week after each completed test.
listings.worth: You get paid $4–10+ per test (10–20 minutes each — roughly $12–30/hour while testing), via PayPal 7 days after each test. Realistic monthly total: $30–100 casually.
smart_moves: Take the practice test seriously — it determines your rating
Speak naturally and continuously during tests
A USB microphone improves audio quality and acceptance rates
Check for tests at off-peak hours when competition is lower
more_info: Why companies pay strangers to click around

Finding usability problems before launch saves companies far more than the $10 they pay you. A single confused user on camera is worth a thousand analytics charts. You're providing genuine value — the pay is honest.

legitimacy.company: UserTesting Inc — San Francisco, founded 2007, publicly traded then acquired 2022
legitimacy.track_record: Fortune 500 client base; 3,400+ Trustpilot reviews at 4.5/5; nearly two decades of continuous payouts
legitimacy.red_flags: None structural. Complaints center on test availability and screener time, not payment failures.

listings.name: Respondent.io
listings.realcost: Nothing. Pays $50–300+ per study for professionals.
listings.short: Yes — Respondent connects companies with professionals for paid research interviews. Typical pay: $75–200 for a 30–60 minute video call about your industry expertise. The highest-paying legitimate research platform online.
listings.catch: It's competitive — most studies target specific professional profiles and fill fast. You need real expertise in something (software, healthcare, finance, teaching — anything) to qualify consistently.
free_includes: Paid research interviews and surveys, typically $75–300+
Payment via PayPal or Payoneer
No fees to participants
Studies from Fortune 500 companies and startups
drawbacks: Most studies target narrow professional profiles
Studies fill in hours — speed matters
Video calls require a camera and quiet space
Income is sporadic, not steady
listings.get_summary: In practice: THEY PAY YOU — $75 to $300+ per interview about your professional field. A single one-hour call can pay more than a week of survey sites.
listings.worth: You get paid $75–300+ per research interview — typically 30–60 minutes, so $100–300/hour while participating. Realistic monthly total: $100–500 if your professional profile matches active studies.
smart_moves: Complete your LinkedIn-connected profile thoroughly — it's how studies find you
Apply to studies the moment you see them — slots fill in hours
Professional experience in any field is the asset; students earn less here
Check daily; the best studies don't send notifications
more_info: Why the pay is so high

Companies pay $150+ per interview because they're buying expertise, not clicks. A 30-minute call with a hospital IT director is worth thousands in product decisions. The high pay reflects the value of your knowledge, not the generosity of the platform.

legitimacy.pays_via: PayPal or Payoneer after study completion
legitimacy.track_record: Clients include Fortune 500 companies and major research firms; Trustpilot 4.2/5
legitimacy.red_flags: None structural. Fraudulent 'studies' impersonating Respondent exist on social media — only accept studies through the actual platform, never through DMs.

listings.name: Fiverr
listings.realcost: An account. Fiverr keeps 20% of every sale.
listings.short: Yes — listing services on Fiverr is free and the platform is genuinely global. You set your rates, deliver digital work, and get paid. The catch: Fiverr takes 20% of every transaction, and the platform's race-to-the-bottom reputation means establishing fair pricing takes effort.
listings.catch: 20% is steep for a marketplace fee. The platform's name ('five-r') anchored expectations at $5 for years, and cheap competition still drives prices down in many categories. Succeeding means either volume or differentiation — not passive income.
free_includes: Free to list and sell services worldwide
Built-in payment processing and dispute resolution
A massive buyer marketplace
Seller levels with increasing perks
drawbacks: 20% commission on every sale
Race-to-the-bottom pricing in crowded categories
Buyer-favoring dispute resolution
Income is earned, not passive — this is freelancing
listings.get_summary: In practice: BUYERS PAY YOU for digital work you deliver — you set your rates, Fiverr keeps 20%. It's a job with a global storefront, not a rewards app.
listings.worth: You get paid what you charge, minus Fiverr's 20% cut. Earnings range from $0 to $10,000+/month depending on skill and effort; median new sellers earn under $500/month. This is freelance income, not passive money.
smart_moves: Start with one service you're genuinely good at — don't scatter
Price above $5 from day one; cheap gigs attract difficult clients
Respond to inquiries within hours — response time affects ranking
Build off-platform skills (portfolio, testimonials) so Fiverr isn't your only channel
more_info: Is this 'easy money'?

No. Fiverr is a job — a flexible, global, start-tonight job, but a job. The 'easy money online' framing is the scam; the platform itself is real. Treat it as freelancing with built-in marketing, not as passive income.

legitimacy.company: Fiverr International Ltd — publicly traded on NYSE (FVRR), founded 2010
legitimacy.pays_via: PayPal, bank transfer, or Fiverr Revenue Card, 14 days after order completion
legitimacy.track_record: Public company with audited financials; millions of completed transactions
legitimacy.red_flags: The platform is legitimate; individual buyers can be difficult. Never take communication or payment off-platform — that's where scams happen.

listings.name: Etsy
listings.limits: $0.20 listing fee + 6.5% transaction fee + payment processing
listings.realcost: Small per-listing fees plus a cut of each sale.
listings.short: Sort of — opening an Etsy shop costs nothing, but selling does: a $0.20 per-listing fee (renewed every 4 months), a 6.5% transaction fee, and payment processing. It's not free to operate, but the barrier to entry is nearly zero.
listings.catch: The fees stack up: listing + transaction + processing can total 10–15% per sale. And Etsy's algorithm changes and ad-pressure mean organic visibility is a moving target. It's a real marketplace with real costs — not a free storefront.
free_includes: Opening a shop and listing products (at $0.20/listing)
Access to Etsy's massive buyer traffic
Built-in payment processing
Shop customization and analytics
drawbacks: Fees total 10-15% per sale when stacked
Intense competition in popular categories
Algorithm changes can crater visibility overnight
Etsy owns the customer relationship, not you
listings.get_summary: In practice: BUYERS PAY YOU for handmade, vintage, or digital goods. Etsy takes its cut on every sale — price your work with the ~10–15% fee stack built in.
listings.worth: You get paid what buyers pay, minus roughly 10–15% in stacked fees ($0.20 per listing + 6.5% transaction + payment processing). Most shops earn under $500/month; digital downloads have the best margins.
smart_moves: Start with digital downloads — no inventory, no shipping, highest margins
Price to include fees from day one; don't discover the 15% cut after your first sale
Build an email list from buyers so you're not 100% dependent on Etsy's algorithm
SEO your titles and tags — Etsy search is the primary discovery channel
more_info: Digital downloads: the Etsy sweet spot

Printable planners, wedding templates, SVG files, resume templates — digital products cost nothing to reproduce and ship instantly. The $0.20 listing fee and percentage cut are the only costs. This is where Etsy genuinely approaches 'easy money' — but the competition is fierce.

legitimacy.company: Etsy Inc — publicly traded on Nasdaq (ETSY), founded 2005
legitimacy.pays_via: Direct bank deposit via Etsy Payments
legitimacy.track_record: Public company; 90+ million active buyers. The low Trustpilot score reflects buyer disputes and seller fee frustration, not payment fraud
legitimacy.red_flags: Legitimate marketplace with real fee complaints. Watch for off-platform payment requests from 'buyers' — always a scam.

listings.name: "Make Money Online" Courses
listings.limits: The course IS the product being sold — not the income it promises
listings.realcost: Hundreds to thousands of dollars, often charged to people who can't afford to lose them.
listings.short: Almost universally, no. The pattern: a guru promises passive income from dropshipping, Amazon FBA, forex, crypto, or affiliate marketing, sells a course for $97–$2,000, and the only proven income stream is selling the course itself. The FTC has prosecuted dozens of these operations.
listings.catch: The business model is the course, not the method taught in it. When pressed for income proof, promoters show their own revenue — which comes from selling the course. The rare legitimate educator exists, but they don't promise easy money and they have verifiable track records outside of course sales.
free_includes: A sales pitch disguised as free value (webinar, masterclass, PDF)
paid_locked: The 'real secrets' — which are more sales pitches for higher-tier courses
drawbacks: The product is the promise, not the delivery
Income screenshots are from course sales, not the taught method
Upsell ladders can run into thousands
Targets people in financial distress
listings.get_summary: In practice: you pay to learn how to sell a course about making money — a closed loop where the only revenue is new believers. The free alternatives on this page teach real skills for $0.
listings.worth: $0 — the value is negative. The free resources on this page (freeCodeCamp, Khan Academy, Google Digital Garage) teach real skills without charging for the privilege.
smart_moves: If the 'proof' is the guru's own income, ask what percentage comes from course sales
Search '[course name] scam' or '[guru name] refund' before buying anything
Legitimate skills training is free: freeCodeCamp, Khan Academy, Google Digital Garage, Coursera audit mode
The FTC's business opportunity rule requires income disclosures — ask for them
more_info: The closed-loop test

Ask one question: does the teacher make money from the method, or from teaching the method? A coding bootcamp instructor who previously built software passes the test. A dropshipping guru whose only verified income is course sales does not. This single question eliminates 90% of 'make money online' courses.
Why the FTC cares

The FTC has brought enforcement actions against dozens of 'business opportunity' sellers for deceptive income claims. The pattern is consistent: exaggerated earnings, hidden costs, and refund barriers. If a course promises specific income figures without an FTC-compliant income disclosure, it's already a red flag.

free_alternatives.name: freeCodeCamp
free_alternatives.why: Learn real, employable skills for $0 — no guru, no upsell

free_alternatives.name: Khan Academy
free_alternatives.why: Complete education, truly free, from a nonprofit

free_alternatives.name: Google Digital Garage
free_alternatives.why: Free digital marketing training with real certificates

listings.name: Swagbucks
listings.limits: Points convert to pennies; most activities pay very little
listings.realcost: Your time, at well below minimum wage for most activities.
listings.short: Sort of — Swagbucks is real and does pay, but most activities (surveys, watching videos, searching) earn pennies per minute. It's legitimate in the sense that gift cards arrive; it's misleading in the sense that 'earn money online' implies something above $2/hour.
listings.catch: The math: most surveys pay 40–100 SB points, which equals $0.40–$1.00 for 10–20 minutes of work. That's $2–6/hour. Watching videos pays even less. The platform works; the hourly rate is the honest problem.
free_includes: Surveys, video watching, shopping cashback, and search rewards
Gift card and PayPal cashout options
A $10 signup bonus (varies)
The dashboard that makes small earnings feel like progress
drawbacks: Most activities pay well below minimum wage
Survey disqualifications waste time with no pay
The points system obscures how little you're earning
Designed to feel more rewarding than it is
listings.get_summary: In practice: THEY PAY YOU, but at $2–6/hour for most activities — well below minimum wage. Real gift cards arrive; the honest problem is the hourly rate, not the legitimacy.
listings.worth: You get paid — but slowly: most surveys pay $0.40–$1.00 for 10–20 minutes, which is $2–6/hour. Realistic monthly total: $20–50 with heavy daily use. The shopping cashback is the only part with a decent return on time.
smart_moves: Use the shopping cashback — that's the only part with a real return on time
Skip video-watching and low-pay surveys; the hourly rate is insulting
Compare your time to Prolific or Respondent, which pay 5–20x more per hour
Cash out at the minimum threshold; don't accumulate points in their system
more_info: Why it feels like more than it is

Points psychology: earning 75 SB 'feels' like earning 75 of something, but it's $0.75. The dashboard gamifies tiny amounts into streaks and goals, which is designed to keep you engaged longer than the pay justifies. It's honest in that it pays; it's manipulative in how it frames the pay.

free_alternatives.name: Prolific
free_alternatives.why: Academic research that pays $8–15/hour — real money for honest answers

free_alternatives.name: Respondent.io
free_alternatives.why: $75–300 per study for professional expertise

legitimacy.company: Prodege LLC — US company operating since 2008
legitimacy.track_record: 43,000+ Trustpilot reviews at 4.0/5; pays reliably, just slowly
legitimacy.red_flags: None on legitimacy — the platform pays. The issue is the hourly rate, which this page states honestly.

sentiment.praised: The volume of positive reviews is real, and so is the payout — reviewers confirm gift cards and PayPal cashouts arrive. The platform is not a scam and the reviews reflect that.
sentiment.complained: The recurring frustration isn't fraud, it's time: survey disqualifications after several minutes of screening, and the slow grind of points that turn out to be worth pennies.
sentiment.takeaway: A 4.0 average that hides a $2–6/hour reality. The reviews measure whether it pays — they don't measure whether it's worth your afternoon. This site measures both.

listings.name: MLMs / Network Marketing
listings.limits: You ARE the customer, not the entrepreneur
listings.realcost: Inventory purchases, monthly minimums, and damaged relationships.
listings.short: No — and this is the most important listing on this page. Multi-level marketing companies (Amway, Herbalife, LuLaRoe, doTERRA, and hundreds more) recruit participants who pay to join, buy inventory, and recruit others. The FTC's own data shows the vast majority of MLM participants lose money.
listings.catch: The pitch frames it as entrepreneurship, but the income comes from recruiting new participants who buy products, not from retail sales to outside customers. When recruitment is the revenue engine, the structure is mathematically identical to a pyramid scheme — which is why the FTC has taken action against dozens of MLMs.
free_includes: A sales pitch about 'being your own boss'
Pressure to buy starter kits and monthly product minimums
paid_locked: Profit — statistically, for the vast majority of participants
drawbacks: The FTC found 99% of MLM participants lose money in studied cases
Requires buying inventory and hitting monthly purchase minimums
Recruiting friends and family strains relationships
Leaving means writing off unsold inventory
listings.get_summary: In practice: you pay to become a customer who recruits other customers. The company profits; the participants at the bottom fund the payouts at the top. This is not a side hustle — it's a payment structure with a product attached.
listings.worth: Negative. The average MLM participant loses money after accounting for required purchases, fees, and time.
smart_moves: If the income requires recruiting, it's not retail — walk away
Search '[company name] income disclosure statement' — most publish them, and the numbers are devastating
Any opportunity that requires you to BUY before you EARN is charging you to work
The FTC's MLM guidance page is the definitive resource — linked above
more_info: The income disclosure math

Companies like Herbalife and LuLaRoe publish income disclosure statements. Reading them reveals that the median annual income for participants is typically $0–$500 before expenses — and expenses (product purchases, minimums, events) regularly exceed that. The top 1% earn real money; the other 99% fund it.
Why it's legal if it looks like a pyramid scheme

The legal line between an MLM and a pyramid scheme is whether real products are sold to real outside customers at real retail prices. Many MLMs exist in the gray zone where participants are the primary customers. When the FTC investigates, companies often settle or restructure rather than face a definitive ruling.

listings.name: Trustpilot
listings.account: Free for consumers; business profile free to claim
listings.limits: Business free tier: ~50 review invitations/month, one basic widget
listings.realcost: Free for consumers entirely. For businesses, the reputation tools start around $200+/month, billed annually upfront.
listings.short: For consumers: yes, completely — reading and writing reviews on the world's largest open review platform is free, no account wall on reading. For businesses: claiming your profile and responding to reviews is free, but the free tier caps at roughly 50 review invitations a month with one basic widget. The real reputation toolkit starts around $200–500/month — billed as a full year upfront, no monthly option.
listings.catch: The structural criticism: paying businesses get better tools for flagging and disputing reviews than free accounts, which critics argue tilts outcomes toward subscribers. Trustpilot denies pay-for-score, and consumers genuinely cannot be charged — but the asymmetry between free and paying businesses is real and documented.
free_includes: Reading all 300+ million reviews, no account needed
Writing reviews free, forever
Businesses: claiming a profile, responding to reviews, ~50 invitations/month, one basic widget
paid_locked: Custom branded widgets and profile design
drawbacks: Paid plans are annual, prepaid, per domain — no monthly exit
Paying businesses get stronger dispute tooling than free ones
Fake reviews slip through despite 4.5M removed in 2024
Your review data lives on their platform; leaving means abandoning it
listings.get_summary: In practice: the consumer side is one of the internet's genuinely free trust utilities. The business side is a $3,000–15,000+/year reputation subscription wearing a free hat.
listings.worth: Consumers: free trust research worth real money in avoided scams. Businesses: paid tiers run $2,400–18,000+/year.
smart_moves: As a consumer, read patterns and recent reviews, not the star number alone
Cross-reference with Google Reviews and one niche platform — no single score is the truth
As a business, the free tier (claim + respond) covers the essentials; the paid jump is steep and annual
Never trust a profile with only perfect reviews posted in bursts — that's the fake-review signature
more_info: The paid-tier bias debate, honestly

Trustpilot is publicly traded (LSE: TRST), removed 4.5 million fake reviews in 2024 — 90% caught automatically — and consumers can't pay to post or businesses to delete. The genuine concern is subtler: subscribing businesses get faster, better-equipped dispute processes than free ones. That's not score-buying, but it's not a level field either. Read any Trustpilot score as one signal from a platform with a business model, not as a verdict.
Reading a profile like a pro

Sort by recent. Read the 1-star and 3-star reviews first — 5-stars are written by enthusiasm, 1-stars by grievance, 3-stars by honesty. Check whether the business responds, and how. A 4.2 with thoughtful responses to complaints beats a silent 4.8.

sentiment.split: Publicly traded (LSE: TRST) · removed 4.5 million fake reviews in 2024
sentiment.praised: The consumer side is genuinely free and genuinely open — anyone can write, anyone can read, and no business can pay to delete a review. The fake-review enforcement is real and published.
sentiment.complained: Businesses on the free tier report that paying subscribers get faster, better-resourced dispute processes. Trustpilot denies pay-for-score, and no evidence contradicts that — but the tooling asymmetry is real.
sentiment.takeaway: Read any Trustpilot score as one signal from a platform with a business model — not as a verdict. That advice applies to the score badges on this site too.

listings.name: Google Reviews
listings.account: Google account to write; none to read
listings.realcost: Nothing in money — for either side. Your review data enriches Google Maps.
listings.short: Yes — the most-used review system on earth is free on both sides: free to read (no account), free to write (any Google account), and free for businesses to receive, display, and respond to via Google Business Profile. No paid tier exists for reviews themselves.
listings.catch: Google's incentive is the ecosystem: reviews make Maps and Search more useful, which sells more ads elsewhere. The absence of a paid reputation tier is exactly why Google Reviews carries more default credibility than platforms that sell business subscriptions.
free_includes: Reading every review without an account
Writing reviews with any Google account
Businesses: receiving, displaying, and responding — all free
Reviews appear directly in Search and Maps results
drawbacks: Moderation is algorithmic and opaque — legitimate reviews sometimes vanish
Review-gating and fake-review sellers target Google constantly
No verified-purchase mechanism — anyone can review anywhere
listings.get_summary: In practice: the default trust check for local businesses worldwide — and the rare review platform with no paid thumb on the scale.
listings.worth: Free on both sides. Local-SEO agencies charge hundreds monthly to manage what the platform itself gives away.
smart_moves: Weight recent reviews heavily — a business changes faster than its average
For local businesses, this matters roughly 10x more than any other platform
Businesses: reply to every review — replies are read by hundreds of future customers
Watch for review bursts (many 5-stars in one week) — the standard fake pattern
more_info: Why no paid tier is the feature

Trustpilot, Yelp, and BBB all sell something to businesses, which creates at least the appearance of tilt. Google Reviews sells nothing on the review side — the incentive is ecosystem quality. That structural neutrality is why a Google rating is the first number most people trust.

listings.name: Yelp
listings.account: Account to write; none to read
listings.limits: Free listing; the ad products are the business
listings.realcost: Free for consumers and for a basic business listing. Yelp's revenue is ads sold to those same businesses — famously aggressively.
listings.short: Yes for consumers — reading and writing is free. For businesses, claiming and managing a listing is free too. The catch is what happens next: Yelp's sales team is legendary for aggressive ad-package calls, and its 'recommendation software' hides a large share of reviews — disproportionately, many owners claim, when they decline to advertise.
listings.catch: Yelp has always denied that advertising affects which reviews get 'recommended,' and courts have sided with Yelp. But the perception problem is permanent: the same company that filters your reviews also sells you ads, and small-business forums overflow with owners describing hidden 5-stars and surfaced 1-stars after declining a sales pitch.
free_includes: Reading all reviews free, no account
Writing reviews with a free account
Businesses: free listing, photos, hours, and responses
paid_locked: Yelp Ads (pay-per-click, the sales team will call)
Removing competitor ads from your own listing page
drawbacks: The recommendation filter hides many legitimate reviews
Aggressive, persistent ad sales calls to listed businesses
Competitors' ads appear on your free listing unless you pay
listings.get_summary: In practice: still the restaurant-and-services standard in big US cities — read it, but know that the reviews you see survived a filter run by the company selling the ads.
listings.worth: Free both sides at the basic level; Yelp Ads run hundreds monthly for businesses that buy in.
smart_moves: Click 'not currently recommended' at the bottom of any listing to see the hidden reviews — often revealing
For restaurants, cross-check Google; Yelp skews harsher
Business owners: you can't opt out of Yelp — claim the listing so you can at least respond
Log every sales call; if ad pressure feels like extortion, the FTC takes complaints
more_info: The filter controversy, both sides

Yelp's software marks reviews 'recommended' or not, and hidden ones don't count toward the score. Yelp says the filter fights fakes and is advertising-blind; a federal court agreed there was no proven manipulation. Thousands of small-business owners remain convinced otherwise. The honest read: the filter catches real fakes AND buries real reviews, and its opacity makes every theory unfalsifiable. Check the hidden reviews yourself — the link is at the bottom of every listing.

listings.name: Better Business Bureau (BBB)
listings.limits: Consumer side free; the A+ next to a business name often reflects paid accreditation
listings.realcost: Free to search and file complaints. Businesses pay ~$500–1,000+/year for accreditation — the seal, not officially the grade.
listings.short: For consumers, yes — searching businesses, reading complaints, and filing disputes is free and genuinely useful. The asterisk is the business side: BBB 'accreditation' is a paid membership, and the organization has spent 15 years living down a 2010 ABC News investigation in which businesses appeared able to buy better grades — including an A rating issued to a fake company.
listings.catch: BBB says grades and accreditation are independent, and it reformed its rating system after the 2010 scandal. But the structure remains: the nonprofit is funded by the businesses it rates. The complaint-mediation service is real value; the letter grade deserves more skepticism than its official-sounding name suggests.
free_includes: Searching any business's complaint history, free
Filing complaints with real mediation — businesses often respond to BBB when they ignore customers
Scam Tracker: a free crowdsourced fraud database
paid_locked: Accreditation (the BBB seal) — a paid annual membership
Enhanced listing features for member businesses
drawbacks: Funded by the businesses it rates — a permanent structural conflict
The 2010 pay-for-grades scandal damaged the brand durably
Grades weight complaint response over complaint substance — a company can be A+ while generating constant complaints it answers politely
listings.get_summary: In practice: use the complaint history and Scam Tracker, which are genuinely free and useful. Treat the letter grade as marketing with a nonprofit accent.
listings.worth: Free complaint mediation has recovered real money for consumers; accreditation costs businesses $500–1,000+/year.
smart_moves: File a BBB complaint when a company ignores you — the mediation channel genuinely moves stuck disputes
Read the complaints themselves, not the grade — the substance is in the text
An A+ means complaints get answered, not that customers are happy
Scam Tracker is underused — search it before wiring money to anyone
more_info: The 2010 investigation, on the record

ABC's 20/20 reported that a Los Angeles BBB chapter issued an A- to a nonexistent business named 'Hamas' after its $425 fee was paid, while Ritz-Carlton and Wolfgang Puck — non-members — carried lower grades. BBB expelled the chapter and removed the accreditation points from its grading formula. The reform was real; the funding structure that created the incentive is unchanged.

listings.name: Glassdoor
listings.account: Required, and it wants your contribution
listings.limits: The 'give-to-get' wall: contribute a review or salary to keep reading
listings.realcost: Free in money; the toll is your own workplace data, and increasingly your identity.
listings.short: Sort of — Glassdoor's famous 'give-to-get' policy lets you read a little before a wall demands you contribute: a company review, a salary figure, or an interview report to unlock continued access. The reviews and salary data are real and useful; the toll is your own information.
listings.catch: Give-to-get is honest in its way — the data you want exists because others paid the same toll. The newer friction is identity: since Glassdoor began requiring verified identities behind the scenes (posts stay anonymous publicly), some users worry about the anonymity chain, especially after accounts were linked to its sister job platform.
free_includes: Company reviews, salary ranges, and interview questions — the workplace transparency standard
Anonymous posting (publicly) with a free account
Job listings alongside the intel
paid_locked: Sustained access without contributing (the wall returns)
Employer-side branding and analytics tools (paid, for companies)
drawbacks: The give-to-get wall interrupts research at the worst moment
Identity verification behind anonymous posts is a trust leap
Employers pay Glassdoor for branding on the same pages job-seekers trust
Salary data skews toward self-selected reporters
listings.get_summary: In practice: the best free look inside a company before you interview — priced in a salary datapoint or review of your current employer.
listings.worth: Salary-negotiation intel here is worth thousands; the toll is one honest contribution.
smart_moves: Pay the toll with a salary figure — most useful to others, least exposing of you
Read reviews by recency and filter by role; a 2019 rant tells you little
Cross-check salaries with levels.fyi for tech roles
Write your own reviews from a personal email, never a work device
more_info: Give-to-get, judged fairly

The wall is why Glassdoor's data exists — pure free-riding would have produced an empty site. As data tolls go, it's proportionate and transparent. The fairer criticism is the employer side: companies pay Glassdoor for enhanced branding on the very pages workers read as neutral, a quieter version of the conflict every review platform carries.

listings.name: Consumer Reports
listings.limits: Nearly everything is paywalled — by design
listings.realcost: About $10/month or $59/year for digital. The paywall is the business model that keeps it ad-free and independent.
listings.short: No — and unusually for this site, that's almost a compliment. Consumer Reports is a nonprofit that accepts no advertising, buys every product it tests at retail, and funds all of it through subscriptions. The ratings people search for — appliances, cars, mattresses — sit behind roughly $10/month.
listings.catch: This is the honest inverse of ad-funded 'free' review sites: you pay money instead of paying with attention and data, and the reviewer has no advertiser to please. The trap isn't the paywall — it's the auto-renewing subscription, which CR runs like everyone else. And the open secret: many public libraries include full Consumer Reports access free.
free_includes: A limited selection of free articles and buying advice
Car recalls and some safety content, free
paid_locked: Virtually all product ratings and test results
Car reliability data — the crown jewels
drawbacks: Auto-renewing subscription with the usual cancellation friction
Digital and print are separate subscriptions
The name gets impersonated by fake 'review' sites constantly
listings.get_summary: In practice: the most independent product testing in America, honestly paywalled — and very often free through your library card anyway.
listings.worth: $59/year buys testing with no advertiser thumb; one avoided bad appliance pays for a decade of it. Library access makes it $0.
smart_moves: Check your library first — many offer full Consumer Reports access free through their digital resources
If subscribing, calendar the renewal; CR auto-renews like the trap trials it would rate poorly
Beware lookalike 'consumer report' sites — the real one is consumerreports.org, and its clones are affiliate farms
Use the free car-recall lookup even without a subscription
more_info: Why the paywall earns respect here

Every 'free' review site on the internet is funded by someone — ads, affiliate links, or business subscriptions — and that someone shapes what gets said. Consumer Reports charges readers directly, buys test products anonymously at retail, and has sued companies rather than soften ratings. The subscription is what independence costs. This site stamps it Not Free because it isn't free; it recommends it anyway.
The library route

Many public library systems include Consumer Reports in their digital databases — full ratings, from home, free with a library card. It joins Libby, Kanopy, and LinkedIn Learning in the library card stack, which remains the most underused bundle of free value in North America.

listings.name: Free Museum Days
listings.realcost: Nothing — most major museums run free admission days or hours, funded by endowments and civic grants.
listings.short: Yes — nearly every major museum offers free admission on select days or evenings. The Smithsonian is free every day. Bank of America cardholders get free 'Museums on Us' weekends at 225+ institutions. Most city art museums have a free weekly evening or monthly Sunday.
listings.catch: The free days are real but crowded, and 'suggested donation' signage at the door is exactly that — suggested, not required. Special exhibitions sometimes carry a separate ticket even on free days.
free_includes: Smithsonian museums — free every day, always
Free weekly or monthly days at most major museums
Bank of America 'Museums on Us' — first full weekend monthly, 225+ museums
'Suggested donation' museums where you set the price, including $0
drawbacks: Free days are the most crowded times to visit
Special exhibits may still cost extra
Some 'free' museums lean hard on donation pressure at entry
listings.get_summary: In practice: world-class art and history for $0 if you time it — check the museum's own calendar, not a third-party aggregator.
listings.worth: A family museum visit runs $60–100 at full price. Free days make it $0.
smart_moves: Google '[museum name] free day' — every museum lists its own
Bank of America/Merrill cardholders: 'Museums on Us,' first full weekend each month
'Suggested donation' means you legally choose the amount — $1 is fine
Weekday mornings on free days are least crowded
more_info: The library museum-pass secret

Beyond free days, many public libraries lend museum passes — reserve one with your library card and get free or deep-discount admission any day. It joins the library card stack alongside Libby and Kanopy as absurdly underused free value.

listings.name: Meetup (free events)
listings.limits: Joining and attending free groups costs nothing; organizing costs money
listings.realcost: Free to attend most groups. Organizing a group costs the host ~$10–25/month, which is where Meetup earns.
listings.short: Mostly — attending Meetup events is free for the vast majority of groups: hiking, board games, language exchange, tech talks, book clubs. The catch sits on the other side: organizers pay $10–25/month to run a group, so some hosts charge small event fees to offset it.
listings.catch: The attendee experience is genuinely free and one of the best ways to find in-person community. The friction is that Meetup has raised organizer fees repeatedly, thinning out free groups as hobbyist organizers give up — so some cities have fewer active free groups than they did five years ago.
free_includes: Joining unlimited groups and RSVPing free
Most casual groups (hiking, games, language, social) charge nothing
In-person community around any interest, in most cities
paid_locked: Some events charge to offset the organizer's fee
drawbacks: Organizer fee increases have thinned free groups
Event quality depends entirely on the volunteer host
Smaller towns have sparse listings
listings.get_summary: In practice: still the best on-ramp to in-person hobby community — free to attend, even if the platform squeezes the people running the groups.
listings.worth: Real-world community and new friendships at $0 — the kind of thing that has no price but changes a year.
smart_moves: Filter for free events and established groups with regular attendance
Show up to three different groups before judging — hosts vary wildly
Public library and Parks & Rec event calendars list free meetups Meetup misses
If a group folds, the organizer probably stopped paying — look for the successor group
more_info: Free alternatives to Meetup itself

Library event calendars, city Parks & Recreation programs, and Facebook Groups host enormous numbers of free gatherings with no organizer fee. Meetup is the most discoverable, but it's not the only — or cheapest — way to find your people.

listings.name: Eventbrite (free events)
listings.limits: Free events are free; the platform charges organizers of paid events
listings.realcost: Nothing for free events. Eventbrite takes a cut only when a ticket has a price.
listings.short: Yes for attendees — Eventbrite lists a huge volume of genuinely free events (workshops, community festivals, author talks, fitness classes, networking), and registering costs nothing. Eventbrite makes its money on service fees attached to paid tickets, not free ones.
listings.catch: For free events there's no catch worth the name. The only friction: some 'free' events are thinly veiled sales seminars (timeshares, financial products, 'free' workshops with an upsell finale) — a pattern worth recognizing before you spend an evening.
free_includes: Browsing and filtering thousands of free local events
Free registration and mobile tickets
Community festivals, talks, classes, and pop-ups
paid_locked: Ticket fees on paid events (borne by attendees or absorbed by organizers)
drawbacks: Some 'free' listings are sales pitches in disguise
Registration doesn't guarantee the event happens
Popular free events fill fast
listings.get_summary: In practice: the widest net for free local happenings — filter to free, scan for the disguised sales seminars, and go.
listings.worth: A city's worth of free entertainment and learning, surfaced in one search.
smart_moves: Filter to 'Free' and sort by date to plan a week of $0 outings
Read the description fully — 'free financial workshop' is usually a sales funnel
Follow organizers whose free events you enjoyed for future ones
Set a location alert for free events in your interests
more_info: Reading a free event listing

Genuine free events describe the actual content: 'Watercolor basics, materials provided.' Disguised sales events stay vague and emphasize the free-ness: 'FREE wealth-building seminar — seats limited!' The vaguer the value and the louder the FREE, the more likely there's a pitch at the end.

listings.name: Groupon
listings.limits: The deals are real discounts, but fine print, fees, and expiry erode the savings
listings.realcost: Not free — it's a discount marketplace. You pay less, but 'less' comes with blackout dates, add-on fees, and use-by windows.
listings.short: No — Groupon isn't free; it's a discount platform, and a legitimate one. The honest question is whether the discounts are as good as they look. Often yes (haircuts, classes, restaurants at 30–56% off), but the fine print — blackout dates, gratuity-not-included, resort fees on travel, tight expiry windows — routinely eats a chunk of the advertised saving.
listings.catch: Groupon is a real marketplace with a real refund policy (the 'Groupon Promise' — three-day refund on local vouchers, and vouchers keep their paid value even after the promo expires). The trap isn't fraud; it's the gap between the headline discount and the true cost after fees, tips, and restrictions. Travel 'Getaways' and mystery vacations are where surprises cluster.
free_includes: Genuine 30–56% discounts on local services you'd buy anyway
The 'Groupon Promise' — 3-day refund window on local vouchers
Voucher retains paid value with the merchant even after the promo expires
Good for trying something once (class, spa, escape room) without full price
paid_locked: The full advertised value once fees, tips, and blackout dates are counted
Flexible dates on travel deals (often restricted)
drawbacks: Fine print (blackout dates, extra fees, gratuity) erodes the headline discount
Tight expiry windows push you to use it or lose the promo value
Travel/Getaways deals carry resort fees, solo-traveler surcharges, and taxes
Some merchants treat Groupon customers as second-tier
listings.get_summary: In practice: a real discount tool, not a free one — best for everyday services you'd pay for anyway, worst for travel packages where fees hide. Always multiply the true cost, not the headline.
listings.worth: Average local discount runs 30–56% — real money if you'd buy the service anyway, illusory if the deal talks you into something you wouldn't.
smart_moves: Read the entire Fine Print section before buying — that's where fees and blackout dates live
Best categories: haircuts, oil changes, fitness classes, restaurants you'd visit anyway
Worst categories: travel Getaways and mystery vacations — fees and restrictions cluster there
Buy within the 3-day refund window in mind; local vouchers keep paid value even after the promo lapses
For gyms: a $5–60 Groupon trial pass beats signing a $50/month contract you might not use
more_info: The Groupon Promise, precisely

Local vouchers (spa, food, services, things-to-do) can be refunded within three days of purchase unless marked final sale — and even after the promotional value expires, the voucher stays worth what you paid at that merchant. Event tickets and seasonal passes are usually final sale. Travel is governed by its own fine print. Knowing which bucket your purchase falls in is the difference between a safe buy and a stuck one.
Why the headline discount lies (a little)

A '$50 for $100 value' massage looks like 50% off. Then: gratuity on the full $100 (not the $50 you paid), a booking limited to weekday afternoons, and a 90-day expiry. The discount is real but smaller than advertised once the extras land. Multiply the true out-the-door cost before deciding — the same discipline this site applies to every 'free' trial.

listings.name: Free Community Events (Library, Parks & Rec)
listings.limits: None — funded by taxes you already pay
listings.realcost: Nothing beyond the property taxes already funding them. This is public money already spent on you.
listings.short: Yes, entirely — public libraries and city Parks & Recreation departments run enormous free programming that almost nobody fully uses: author talks, kids' story time, film screenings, concerts in the park, fitness classes, craft workshops, tech help, tax help, and lending of everything from tools to museum passes.
listings.catch: There is no catch. The only reason this isn't obvious is that public institutions don't advertise the way businesses do — so the single best source of free local leisure sits behind a plain municipal website most people never check.
free_includes: Author talks, concerts, film nights, and workshops — free
Kids' programming: story time, STEM clubs, summer reading
Free fitness and yoga in parks (many cities)
Tool libraries, seed libraries, museum-pass lending, and more
drawbacks: Municipal websites are often ugly and hard to search
Popular programs (kids' classes) fill fast
Programming varies widely by city and library system
listings.get_summary: In practice: the highest-value, lowest-profile free leisure in any town — funded by taxes you already pay, listed on a website you've never visited.
listings.worth: Hundreds of dollars a year in classes, entertainment, and lending — already paid for by your taxes, whether you use it or not.
smart_moves: Bookmark your library's and Parks & Rec event calendars — check monthly
Get a library card even if you 'don't read' — the events and lending are the hidden value
Ask a librarian what's underused; they'll point you to passes and programs nobody claims
Summer is peak free programming: outdoor concerts, movies, festivals
more_info: Why this beats every paid platform

Meetup charges organizers, Groupon charges you, Eventbrite hosts sales pitches. The library charges nothing and hosts no upsell because it isn't a business — it's a service you've already funded. The only cost is the ten minutes it takes to find the calendar. This is the closest thing to genuinely free leisure that exists.

listings.name: "Free Vacation" Prizes & Sweepstakes
listings.limits: You never entered the contest you 'won'
listings.realcost: You pay in 'taxes and fees' upfront, and often in stolen credit-card data. The vacation frequently doesn't exist.
listings.short: No — this is one of the oldest scams on the internet, and the FTC is unambiguous: a legitimate company never asks you to pay for a free prize. The pattern: an email, call, or postcard says you won a vacation you never entered a contest for, then demands upfront 'taxes,' 'insurance,' or 'processing fees' — usually by wire transfer, gift card, or crypto, the three payment methods you can never claw back.
listings.catch: The tell is universal: if you have to pay to claim it, it isn't free. Real prizes have taxes you pay to the IRS directly, never to the company issuing the prize. Vague details are the second tell — a real offer names the hotel and cruise line; a scam says '5-star resort' and won't get specific. Once you pay, the vacation evaporates and they have your card.
free_includes: Nothing. There is no vacation.
paid_locked: The 'prize' — which never materializes after you pay the fees
drawbacks: Demands upfront payment for a 'free' prize (always a scam)
Insists on wire transfer, gift card, or crypto — untraceable by design
Won't name the specific hotel, cruise line, or dates
Manufactures urgency: 'claim within 48 hours'
Harvests your credit-card number under cover of 'processing'
listings.get_summary: In practice: not a deal, a data-and-money grab. The BBB Scam Tracker logged over 9,000 travel scams by mid-2025. If you didn't enter, you didn't win.
listings.worth: Negative — victims lose the upfront fees and often their card details. The FTC says a legitimate prize never requires payment.
smart_moves: A free prize that requires payment is, by definition, not free — walk away
Never pay taxes or fees on a 'prize' to the company offering it — real prize taxes go to the IRS
Never pay any travel offer by wire, gift card, or crypto — use a credit card so you can dispute
If they won't name the hotel and dates, there is no hotel or dates
Report it to the FTC (reportfraud.ftc.gov) and the FBI's IC3
more_info: The three untraceable payment methods

Wire transfer, gift cards, and cryptocurrency share one trait: once sent, the money is gone forever with no chargeback. Any 'company' that insists on one of these for a vacation is telling you it's a scam. Legitimate travel businesses take credit cards precisely because cards offer dispute protection — which fraudsters need to avoid.
Why 'you won' works

The scam exploits a real cognitive glitch: winning feels true even when you know you entered nothing. The excitement short-circuits the obvious question — how did I win a contest I never entered? Naming that gap out loud is the whole defense.

free_alternatives.name: Free Community Events (Library, Parks & Rec)
free_alternatives.why: Genuinely free local outings, no fees, no pitch

free_alternatives.name: Free Museum Days
free_alternatives.why: Real free experiences with no strings

listings.name: Timeshare 'Free Stay' Presentations
listings.limits: The free nights are real; the cost is a high-pressure sales day and the risk of a $24,000 decision
listings.realcost: The stay can be genuinely free — but you 'pay' with hours of aggressive sales pressure and the risk of signing a decades-long contract.
listings.short: Technically sometimes yes — and that's what makes it dangerous. Many timeshare 'free weekend' offers really do give you the nights. The catch isn't fraud; it's the mandatory multi-hour sales presentation engineered to pressure you into a timeshare averaging $24,000 plus perpetual maintenance fees. The free stay is bait for a decision the FTC and BBB warn about constantly.
listings.catch: This sits in an honest gray zone: legitimate companies, real free nights, legal contracts — but a sales process specifically designed to override your judgment with urgency, luxury staging, and 'today only' pricing. If you can attend, collect the free nights, and walk out having signed nothing, you win. Most people can't, because the pitch is professionally built to make walking out feel impossible.
free_includes: The free nights or resort stay, if you attend the presentation
Sometimes free amusement-park tickets or meal vouchers
paid_locked: Your afternoon (2–4+ hours of scripted high-pressure sales)
Your judgment, which the presentation is designed to erode
drawbacks: The 'short presentation' runs hours by design
Timeshares average $24,000+ plus perpetual annual maintenance fees
'Book anytime' promises collide with blackout dates and booking restrictions
The rescission (cancellation) window is often just a few days — easy to miss
Claimed rental income and resale value are largely illusory
listings.get_summary: In practice: not a scam, but a trap for your judgment. Free nights are real; the danger is a five-figure decision made under engineered pressure. Attend only if you're certain you can say no five times and leave.
listings.worth: The free stay has real value ($200–500) — but only if you sign nothing. The downside risk is a $24,000+ contract you may spend years trying to exit.
smart_moves: Decide before you arrive that you will not buy, no matter what — and tell your partner the same
Ask why 'today only' pricing can't be available next week; the answer reveals the pressure
If you do sign, use the rescission period immediately — it's only a few days and starts at signing
Never believe rental-income or resale promises; timeshares are famously hard to sell
Add up the true lifetime cost (price + decades of maintenance fees) before signing anything
more_info: The rescission period is your real protection

Most timeshare contracts include a short cooling-off window — often just 3–7 days — during which you can cancel with a full refund. Complaints cluster on people who waited until they got home and missed it. If you sign anything, cancel in writing immediately, and know that later 'exit companies' promising to free you are their own thriving scam category charging thousands upfront.
Why we stamped this Free-ish, not Fake

The free nights are usually real, so this isn't fraud — but the 'free' hides a cost (hours of pressure) and a catastrophic downside risk (a five-figure impulse contract). That's the exact shape of a Free-ish verdict: a real free thing, structured to move you toward a very expensive one.

listings.name: House Sitting & Home Exchange
listings.limits: Genuinely near-free travel lodging — but the platforms charge an annual membership
listings.realcost: The accommodation is free; the platform that connects you charges ~$130–170/year in membership.
listings.short: Close to it — this is the legitimate version of 'free vacation.' House-sitting platforms (TrustedHousesitters, others) and home-exchange networks let you stay in someone's home for free — you watch their pets or swap your own home — while the only cost is the platform's annual membership, roughly $130–170. No hidden fees, no sales pitch, no scam.
listings.catch: The 'free' is real: you genuinely pay nothing for the lodging, which is the biggest travel expense. The honest asterisk is the membership fee and the actual responsibility — you're caring for someone's pets and home, which is a real commitment, not a vacation from all duties. But for animal-loving flexible travelers, it's the closest thing to free accommodation that exists legitimately.
free_includes: Free accommodation worldwide in exchange for pet/house sitting
Home exchange: swap your home for someone else's, no money changes hands
No booking fees or commissions on the stays themselves
A genuine, scam-free model used by hundreds of thousands
paid_locked: The annual membership (~$130–170) to access listings
Premium tiers with cancellation insurance and priority
drawbacks: Annual membership required before you can book anything
Real responsibility: pets and homes need genuine care
Competitive — desirable sits in popular cities fill fast
Requires flexibility on dates and destinations
listings.get_summary: In practice: the real answer to 'free vacation.' You pay a membership and take on genuine pet/house-care duties, and in return the largest travel cost — lodging — drops to zero. Legitimate, popular, and honest about what it is.
listings.worth: Free lodging can save $100–250+/night. A single week-long sit can pay back the annual membership many times over.
smart_moves: Build a strong profile with references before applying to competitive sits
Start with a short local sit to earn your first review
Read the pet-care requirements honestly — this is a commitment, not a free ride
Home-exchange networks (no pet duties) suit homeowners who'd rather swap than sit
more_info: Why this is the legitimate 'free vacation'

Every scam on this page dangles free travel to extract money or data. House-sitting inverts it: a transparent membership, a real service you provide, and genuinely free lodging in return. The largest single travel expense — a hotel — goes to zero, honestly. It's not effortless, but it's real, which no 'free vacation prize' ever is.

listings.name: "Free Phone" Carrier Deals
listings.limits: The phone is 'free' only if you stay 24–36 months on a premium plan
listings.realcost: $2,000–2,700+ in required plan payments over 36 months, plus taxes and a $35 activation fee upfront.
listings.short: No. The phone costs $0 only in the sense that the carrier applies a monthly bill credit equal to your monthly device payment — for 24 to 36 months, and only while you stay on a specific premium unlimited plan. Leave early, downgrade, or change lines and the credits stop instantly while the remaining device balance comes due.
listings.catch: The plan requirement is where the money actually goes. AT&T's free-iPhone offers require an unlimited plan starting at $75.99/month — that's about $2,735 over the 36-month term, before the $35 activation fee and the sales tax on the phone's full retail price, both due upfront. Even the minimum $65.99 plan totals roughly $2,376, over $1,200 more than simply buying the phone outright. This is the old two-year contract wearing a new name.
free_includes: A new flagship phone, at $0/month net — as long as every condition holds for the full term
paid_locked: Your freedom to switch carriers for 2–3 years
The cheaper plan you'd otherwise have chosen
The remaining bill credits, forfeited the moment you leave
drawbacks: Credits spread over 24–36 months; leave early and you forfeit the rest AND owe the device balance
Requires an expensive unlimited tier — the plan premium is the real price of the 'free' phone
Sales tax on full retail plus ~$35 activation, due upfront
Carriers pay well below market for trade-ins — Swappa found a phone earning $200 in carrier credits often sells for $350–450 privately
Autopay and paperless usually mandatory; skipping them adds $5–10/month
Often requires adding a new line — $40–90/month for three years
listings.get_summary: In practice: a 0% loan on a phone, where the carrier promises to pay it off for you — provided you rent their most expensive plan for three years and never leave. The phone is bait; the plan is the product.
listings.worth: Negative for most people. A 'free' $1,199 iPhone on AT&T's $75.99 plan costs about $2,735 over 36 months. Buying the phone unlocked and running a $35 prepaid plan runs roughly $2,459 — and you can walk away any month.
smart_moves: Do the 36-month math before signing: (required plan × 36) + taxes + $35 activation. Compare it to (phone retail + your preferred cheaper plan × 36)
Sell your old phone privately — Swappa and buyback firms routinely pay 30% more than carrier trade-in credit
The deal only makes sense if ALL of these are true: you're staying on that carrier 3 years, you wanted that plan anyway, and your trade-in is a recent flagship
If there's any chance you'll move, switch jobs, or find better coverage, bill credits become a trap — the balance comes due
Buying unlocked plus a prepaid or MVNO plan is almost always cheaper and always more flexible
more_info: What a bill credit actually is

The phone goes on a 24- or 36-month installment plan at 0% interest. Instead of discounting it, the carrier credits your bill each month by roughly the amount of the device payment, netting $0. The credits are conditional on keeping a qualifying plan on that carrier for the full term. Cancel at month 14 of 36 and the credits stop that day — while the unpaid device balance, often $800 or more on a flagship, becomes due. Nothing was ever discounted; the discount was a promise contingent on your loyalty.
When it genuinely does pay off

Swappa's honest framing is worth repeating: the carrier route can win if four things are all true — you were upgrading to a flagship anyway, you have no intention of switching carriers for 2–3 years, your trade-in is a recent flagship in good condition, and the required plan is one you'd have paid for regardless. If all four hold, the math can legitimately favor the deal. If even one fails, you're subsidizing a phone you could have bought outright.
The trade-in gap

Carriers typically offer around $300 for an iPhone 14 Pro Max; third-party buyback services routinely pay roughly 30% more for the same device. In one comparison of 14 models, a buyback company outpaid carriers on 11 of them, sometimes by $80–150. Your old phone is worth real cash — just not to the carrier that's using it as leverage.

free_alternatives.name: Amazon Trade-In
free_alternatives.why: Sell your old device for real credit instead of locked-in bill credits

listings.name: checkmysite.pro
listings.limits: Unlimited scans — no daily cap, no queue
listings.realcost: Nothing. There is no paid tier to be upsold to.
listings.short: Yes — checkmysite.pro grades any website on security and speed with no signup, no account, no card, and no scan limit. Every check runs in full every time; there is no premium tier holding results back, because the tool doesn't have one.
listings.catch: The honest one: a free tool with no paid tier is a front door for a web studio. You're seeing the studio's competence rather than paying for the scan. That's a fair trade and a common model — but know that the business goal is that some fraction of people who scan a broken site go on to hire someone to fix it.
free_includes: SSL/TLS certificate and encryption checks
Security header analysis (CSP, HSTS, nosniff, frame protection, referrer, permissions)
Exposure checks — software version leakage, cookie flags, error messages
Email trust: SPF, DKIM and DMARC records
Lighthouse-based mobile load speed
Page basics — viewport, language, character set, markup
Findability: SEO tags, indexing, and crawler blocks
Plain-English explanation of every result, not just a score
Unlimited scans of any site — yours or a competitor's
drawbacks: Point-in-time scan — no continuous monitoring or alerting when something breaks
No historical tracking, so you can't chart improvement over time without re-scanning manually
Scans the page you give it, not a full-site crawl
A newer tool without the long track record of established scanners
listings.get_summary: In practice: paste an address, get graded security and speed results in seconds, with each finding explained in language you can act on. The explanations are the actual product — most free scanners give you a number and charge for the reasons.
listings.worth: Free. A comparable security-and-performance audit from an agency runs $200–1,000, and most 'free' scanners show a score then paywall the findings.
smart_moves: Scan a competitor's site as well as your own — the comparison tells you more than either score alone
Fix the email trust findings first (SPF, DKIM, DMARC); they're the cheapest to resolve and protect your name, not just your site
Security headers are usually a single config file on most hosts — the highest-value/lowest-effort items on any report
Re-scan after every change; the score is only meaningful against a current build
Pair it with Google Search Console for indexing data this tool can't see from the outside
more_info: Why 'no paid tier' is the thing to notice

Most free website checkers run the full scan, show you the grade, then charge to reveal which specific items failed and why — the score is the teaser and the reasons are the product. This one publishes the reasons. That single structural difference is the whole reason it earns a Truly Free stamp rather than a Free-ish one: there is no held-back tier for a paid version to unlock.
What a scan can and can't tell you

External scanners read what your server sends a visitor: certificates, headers, DNS records, page markup, and measured load time. That covers a genuinely useful slice of risk. What no external scan can see is your admin passwords, your plugin versions behind a login, your backups, or your database. A clean report means the front door is well fitted — it isn't a full security audit, and no free tool is.
listings.disclosure: Shares an owner with Verified Free. The verdict rests on a fact anyone can check in ten seconds — there is no paid tier.

deals.name: Canva Pro

deals.how: Apply at canva.com/education or canva.com/canva-for-nonprofits with verification
deals.worth: ~$120/year — the full Pro plan with premium assets, background remover, brand kits, and 100GB storage
deals.caveat: Requires verification; processing takes a few days. The education program covers teachers and classrooms, not individual students.

deals.name: GitHub Education

deals.who: Students 13+ at accredited institutions

deals.caveat: Requires re-verification annually. Individual partner offers have their own terms and expiry windows.

deals.name: Figma Education
deals.summary: Verified students and educators get Figma's Professional plan — normally $12–20/user/month — free for up to two years.
deals.who: Students and educators at accredited institutions
deals.how: Apply at figma.com/education with school email verification
deals.worth: ~$144–240/year — unlimited files, team libraries, and full version history
deals.caveat: Lasts up to two years; must re-verify. Graduates lose access when student status ends.

deals.name: Notion for Education

deals.who: Students and teachers with a .edu or equivalent school email
deals.how: Sign up with your school email; the upgrade applies automatically
deals.worth: ~$96/year — unlimited blocks for teams, 30-day version history, and unlimited file uploads
deals.caveat: Automatic via school email domain; no application form needed. Access lasts while the school email is active.

deals.name: Apple Music Student
